The nesting of (defer|allow)_cancellation seems particularly tricky to me.  Consider this example::

    async def coro():
            async with defer_cancellation:
                    statements
                    ...
                    async with allow_cancellation:
                            await some_blocking()

    async def sometask():
            async with defer_cancellation:
                    await coro()

Does the `allow_cancellation` block actually allow the delivery of a cancellation or not to the `some_blocking()` operation?  Especially in light of the `sometask()` coroutine explicitly not allowing cancellation at the outer level. 

As currently implemented, it seems that the above code could be cancelled because the internal check for cancellation only looks at the top-most level of nesting.   I'm now wondering if it should consider all of the levels.  That is, cancellation is only allowed if ALL of the levels allow it.

Thoughts?

I think I have a better idea of what is happening here and possibly how to fix it.  Short version:  The keyboard interrupt is being caught in a strange spot and the kernel is not able to cleanly cancel things. Need to spend some time fiddling with it.

I'm not sure this would fix the starvation issue being discussed earlier.   I think the case that @njsmith was worried about involved I/O operations that completed immediately, never trapping into the kernel.   Since they never trap into the kernel, the scheduler simply wouldn't run.    This might explain the unchanged performance on the echo benchmark.

I guess we're sort of threading a fine line with all of this.  Ideally, I'd like to make Curio work with as little fuss as possible--especially for common cases.   For some of the nasty corner cases, maybe making a best effort to steer people in the right direction is about the best we can hope for right now.

I've been thinking a lot about this issue and have concluded that it is basically an application level concern that can be solved with proper forethought and synchronization.   For example, the only time I could even trigger this condition is in code that simultaneously tried to send/recv right at the start of an SSL connection using multiple tasks. For example:

    async def incoming(client):
            while True:
                    data = await client.recv(100000)
                    ...

    async def outgoing(client):
            await client.send(b'hello\n')
            ...

    async def handle_connnection(client, addr):
           t1 = await spawn(incoming, client)
           t2 = await spawn(outgoing, client)

One of the tasks crashes with an exception due to two tasks simultaneously read-waiting on the file descriptor (the send waiting to read in order to process the client handshake).  Yes, it's a problem, but it's easily fixed with an Event. For example::

    async def incoming(client, hello_evt):
            await hello_evt.wait()
            while True:
                    data = await client.recv(100000)
                    ...

    async def outgoing(client, hello_evt):
            await client.send(b'hello\n')
            await hello_evt.set()
            ...

    async def handle_connnection(client, addr):
           evt = Event()
           t1 = await spawn(incoming, client, evt)
           t2 = await spawn(outgoing, client, evt)

Although it's a tricky corner case, I'd rather leave it out of the Curio kernel proper.  Curio rightfully reports an exception if something weird happens.   As far as I'm concerned, anything related to SSL renegotiation probably needs to be carefully coordinated at the application level anyways.   For example HTTP/2 seems to forbid it entirely. 

Bottom line: I'd much rather err on the side of keeping the Curio kernel simple. As long as it allows the problem to ultimately be solved at the application level, I'm okay with that.

Dear @szaghi 

The heart of the matter is the separation of rules for the project and machine-specific rules, detailing things such as which compilers to use (mpifort, mpiifort, whatever these are named), compiler flags and locations of libraries.

Build rules and general configurations should be under git, but machine-specific configurations (and debug options etc) not (in my opinion). For this some templates can be provided, but it is quite inconvenient to have a 'dirty' makefile in your directory, both with regard to accidentally commiting and with changing things in the project-wide makefile.

To make this your approach would be sufficient, but different section names are unnecessary.
I think it would be best to only give a warning if a key does not exist, in that way libraries can be disabled without changing the main fobos file.

Thank you for writing this proposal. Please do not feel hurried, I have implemented a workable GNU Make solution for now.

Best,
Daan

You can also create a file that is nothing else but a glue to have all libraries in the same file (assuming they do not have conflicting names - better would be to use the only keyword indeed).
```
! mylib.f90
module mylib
use mod1, mod2  ! contained in file1, file2
end module mylib
```
and then use FoBiS.py to track the dependencies:
```
FoBiS.py build -target mylib.f90 -mklib static
```

About the use of wildcards and how to find files to parse, maybe the example of `grep` would be an inspiring guideline. `grep` does namely exactly that: parse for files that match some regular expression: `grep regexp file1 file2 direc1/*`  or `grep regexp dir1/* -r` for a recursive search. 

A last comment about FoBiS.py structure: I think the modules (the actually meaningful part) should be more clearly separated from the files (where the information is searched). I would find it more straightforward if the source files were only appearing in one location, for example indeed the -src flag, or even as for grep, as a free list of files and directory at the end of the command. The other flags (e.g. target) should only refer to module or executable names: one the one side the pool of files, on the other what to do with the modules and executable found there.

What's happening is that python prefers to look in relative paths before absolute paths. Since you can import noise from the repo directory, that means it's probably already in your python path.

Say this repository lives in `foo/noise/` and now you are in `foo/` if you go up a directory, then python will see that the local directory `noise/` has an `__init__.py` file and try to import the directory instead of the module installed in your python path. Since this module requires the `_noise` and `_simplex` C extensions to be compiled, this wont work.

Remove or rename this repo directory from your project and use pip. If you really want to install from source, run `python setup.py build` to build locally and `python setup.py install` to install into your python path.

The [Incremental export start time](https://developer.zendesk.com/rest_api/docs/core/incremental_export#start-time) uses Unix epoch time (from UTC). The actual problem is that `strftime("%s")` uses the systems timezone regardless of what timezone the datetime could have.

> Python doesn't actually support %s as an argument to strftime (if you check at http://docs.python.org/library/datetime.html#strftime-and-strptime-behavior it's not in the list), the only reason it's working is because Python is passing the information to your system's strftime, which uses your local timezone.

Currently pantable is designed to be a "simple filter", meaning that the file itself is a self-contained filter (except for the use of backport.csv in py2) that requires nothing but Python standard library plus panflute. The idea is that one can just copy and paste it in pandoc-data-dir and run it. So it is very easy to maintain one's own fork of this for now.

If I find a nice clean way to expand the functionality of pantable (such as providing a cli tool), I might refactor things a bit that it no longer would be self-contained. But by then it should be easy to do what you are asking and don't need to fork it.

Hi @prem9753! You can do this pretty easily, the sequence of commands would just be:
```
# Start ZAP
zap-cli start
# Open a URL through the proxy to add it to the site tree
zap-cli -v open-url http://127.0.0.1/index.php?foo=bar
# Save the session to a file
zap-cli -v session save /path/to/file/some-session-name.session
# Shutdown ZAP
zap-cli -v shutdown
```

This will save the session to the file located at `/path/to/file/some-session-name.session`. Then if you want to use that session later, you can simply load it into ZAP (if ZAP is running) using:
```
zap-cli -v session load /path/to/file/some-session-name.session
```

I hope that helps!

Provided you are building a custom authentication module and having issues configuring the variables, the best thing to do would be local settings file of built in as part of the main python application where you are preforming the actual bind. Basically you would import ldap3 here and implement the required function get_user(user_id) and authenticate(request, **credentials). ```python
```python
from cryptography.x509 import load_pem_x509_certificate as load_pem
from cryptography.hazmat.backends import default_backend


def load_public_from_x509(x509_filename):
    """Opens an X509 cert and returns its public key"""
    with open(x509_filename, 'r') as cert_file:
        cert = load_pem(cert_file.read().encode(),
                        default_backend())
        key = cert.public_key()
    return key
```

> `return self.app.conf.get(self.max_loop_interval_config) or DEFAULT_MAX_INTERVAL`


instead. That way **DEFAULT_MAX_INTERVAL** will be picked up and used  if not overridden.

Not sure if it makes sense to define a default `beat_max_loop_interval` for the scheduler if we can override it on the command line or with **CELERY_BEAT_MAX_LOOP_INTERVAL** setting. Probably a matter of taste...

OK, I think I've finally got a handle on the underlying issue I have here.

The thing that was bugging me was `proto='tcp', fail_silently=True`. It's dangerous, or requires that `fail_silently` doesn't do what it says on the tin.

The root of the problem is: UDP clients are stateless(/connectionless), and TCP clients would be stateful(/connection-mode). That manifests in a couple of big ways:

- Errors. UDP clients just can't raise very many. It doesn't matter what `fail_silently` says: you will never get a exception from the vast majority of things that can go wrong. UDP clients don't even care if the target exists, let alone if they can connect to it.
- Thread safety. Since the underlying connection is stateless, UDP clients are trivially thread-safe. The only thread-unsafe part of the Pipeline is storing the unsent data, the actual sending could be safely interleaved with another thread without a problem.

I started out with the position that changing the underlying transport shouldn't change the application semantics, but what I come around to is: that's nonsense. It literally doesn't make sense. But I still think that changing _just_ the transport shouldn't. Changing one kwarg, especially for the default clients, shouldn't require re-working how an application uses the client.

So what I'm coming around to is: it should be possible, but harder. Supporting TCP does belong in this library, but not in the same object.

What do you think about a `TCPStatsClient` (or a better name) class? It can probably subclass the current `StatsClient` class—I think there are plenty of hooks already to support Pipelines, judging from the existing code here.

This would allow a bunch of things:

- `StatsClient` is thread safe, but `TCPStatsClient` is not (much like Pipelines). This avoids having to hide stateful connections in threadlocals. Applications can still create threadlocal clients if they need to.
- `StatsClient` swallows the basically zero errors it can produce, but `TCPStatsClient` does not, and makes the application decide what to do. That way we don't have to do any hidden retries, and we respect the spirit of the "reliable transport" idea.
- `TCPStatsClient` can expose `reconnect` and `close` methods that don't make sense for `StatsClient`.
- `TCPStatsClient` can have additional constructor kwargs that don't make sense or are unnecessary for `StatsClient`, like `timeout`, and `debug` (I've always maintained it's unnecessary for the UDP client: since you're already changing constructor args, just point it at localhost. But for a TCP client, skipping the connection and actual sending matters). It could even drop `maxudpsize` and just rely on `sendall` for Pipelines (eventually, it would require a little refactoring).
- Opting for TCP is possible but a bigger choice. It makes more sense to have different application semantics when you're creating an entirely different kind of object.

I don't really imagine having `TCPStatsClient` instances in the `statsd.defaults` modules. Those are intended for super-common cases, but anything else—including TCP—is expected to create its own client.

Sorry I've been a little slow on this one. I had to write like 4 or 5 answers before I actually figured out what I was getting at.

> I'll use an object internal dict() to keep the sockets, the keys will be the thread's TIDs

Before picking a solution, I want to make sure we're very clear on what the new guarantee is. Personally, I still want `StatsClient` objects to be thread-safe, and assume that `Pipeline` objects will continue to not be. What happens when a thread creates a `Pipeline` from a shared `StatsClient`?

A lot of this complexity is exactly why I am still not yet convinced this is a good idea. Early on there were attempts to do more complex threading management and the whole library got better when they were taken out. How big a win is TCP support vs the code cost?

(I think a `threading.local()` instance would beat an internal `dict()`, if we decide to go this route.)

> I will not loop or sleep in the Statsd client. I will rather call connect() in send() instead of init() if the socket is not already connected. If an open connection has died I'll try to reconnect only once on send(). If a connect() fails due to connection refused or connection timed out the exception will be raised to be handled by the user, so if the user uses TCP he should wrap the send() in a try: except:.

That dramatically changes the contract of the library from "doesn't raise" to "might raise." That's huge.

It's subtle, but this was a very opinionated decision: that it's better for the application to work than for instrumentation to be 100% reliable. An argument can be made that it's the wrong decision, or that it should be left up to the developer, but an implicit switch based on the transport isn't the way to do it. (If anything, an `__init__` kwarg like `fail_silently=True` would be the right way.) Developer A may not know that DevOps B decided to change the transport, and that they now need to try/except every call. Conversely, DevOps B probably didn't audit the code base to catch everything when the network transport requirements changed.

I may be misunderstanding, in which case correct me if I'm wrong, but from what I understood:

In general, I am against letting exceptions be raised in the validation. If the object to be matched (not the pattern, the supplied object) is not string-like, validation should fail with a `SchemaError`. That's because, if I expect a pattern that matches `"hello"` and the user supplies `None`, I want validation to fail as normal. In general, while validating, I don't care about specifically what the user supplied, I only care if it validates or not. Therefore, I would prefer that you not let the `TypeError` be raised, but at least re-raise the error message with a `SchemaError`.

As for the tests, I don't think that searching for a string-like type that's not a string is necessary, unless you have a specific case you want to test. If it's obscure enough that you're having trouble finding a type to test it, you should probably let it fail :smile:. In practice, it will either work or it will raise a `SchemaError`, which is the right behaviour anyway.

There is another issue hidden in there: What if there is no payload (ie. in a GET-request)? Currently mohawk ends up interpreting this as an empty string, then calculating the hash and using that for the MAC-calculation.

---

> If it doesn't fail, you'd still need to calculate your own hash and re-calculate the MAC (which means you'd always be calculating a MAC twice) because otherwise an attacker could just swap out a known good hash in the request header and trick your server.

IMHO the upstream implementation doesn't calculate the MAC twice. The implementation does:

1. Calculate the MAC using the header supplied hash -> MacMismatch if they don't match
2. Calculate the hash using the actual payload and compare it to the header-supplied hash -> MisComputedContentHash if they don't

There is no need to do another calculation of the MAC.

See [server.js line 165-183](https://github.com/hueniverse/hawk/blob/master/lib/server.js#L165).

I'm a longtime user and fan of Python 2 myself, Gunther. In fact, the initial public release of boltons didn't even have Python 3 compatibility out of the box.

Which quickly brings me to my first and only point: this is not a Python 2 vs Python 3 issue. JSON simply does not ever store bytes. It only supports ints, floats, lists, maps, nulls, and _text_.

Furthermore, JSON can be encoded as UTF-8, 16, or 32, meaning blanket re-encoding of the text objects may not only be an undue performance hit, it may also be not the same bytes you got.

I feel your pain. The str type is a good one, and [I've fought battles trying to protect its legacy too](http://bugs.python.org/issue24019), but this isn't one of them. Technically it's possible. However it depends on what you consider a file.

If you're talking about only the file path then yes, you can do it without going through any hoops. But if you are talking about the *content* of the file and you are working with binary files using the JSON storage you'd have to encode it in some ascii-only format, e.g. base64 so that the JSON library, working only with UTF8 can serialise it. Case in point:

```python
>>> from base64 import b64encode
>>> import json
>>> json.dumps({'a': b'a'})
...
TypeError: b'a' is not JSON serializable
>>> json.dumps({'a': b64encode(b'a').decode()})
'{"a": "YQ=="}'
```

Although for larger stuff it is not practical. You should [write a custom storage class](https://tinydb.readthedocs.io/en/latest/extend.html#write-a-custom-storage) that uses BSON or some format capable of handling binary data. Although if you insist on using the JSON storage, you should use [a custom middleware](https://tinydb.readthedocs.io/en/latest/extend.html#write-a-custom-middleware) that handles binary data automatically.

Hi,
To rename channel, there should be a method called rename_channel() available.
Indeed, when there is duplicate channel name in the same file, it appends the data group number to it to make it unique. If ithis would not exist and because the file content (all channels) is flattened (mdf file content is more structured like a tree), you would write different data in the same 'container' or dict key = channel name, overwriting them, loosing data, which one do not want.
There has been long thinking on what is best mdf object definition for python and this flattened way seemed the most simple, easiest to access/analyse data and close to bare python but it brings then some complications like this one.
This number appending happens in mdfinfo3&4, info class.
For instance mdfinfo4, in readCNBlock,  around line 1578 or 1581, so you could change the behaviour relatively easily and append source instead.
However, I would not advise it because: 
- reading by default SIBlocks (source information blocks), will have performance implication for files having many channels, reading xmls having a cost.
- some channels could not have source information and have same name, allowed by standard
- arrays could have same names and they cannot have source information
- the same signal could be recorded at different sampling rate but same source and same name, causing troubles later, which one to consider globally ? (However, I do not think this is practically done by logging tools)
--> the standard enforces uniqueness in the complete file with (channel name, source, path, group name/number, group source source and group path). mdfreader implementation is not exactly this but having (cn, cg, dg) is equivalent, easiest (I am also lazy sometimes), and performing.
By the way, you can understand raster, channel groups or data group (depends if sorted file or not) though the masterChannelList attribute of mdf class : each of its keys correspond to a master and its value is a list of related channel names.

I don't recommend using `env(...)` directly in your application code. Better to parse your env vars once in a settings file.

```python
# settings.py

DEBUG = env.bool("DEBUG", default=False)
```

```python
# app.py

if settings.DEBUG:

    #...
```

environs doesn't support the use case of calling `env(...)` multiple times for the same env var, which is why we decided to [drop the schema API found in django-environ and envparse](https://github.com/rconradharris/envparse/issues/12#issuecomment-214729943).

> `0/0=0` here is consistent with sklearn, so I'd recommend keeping it as is unless there are strong arguments in favor of changing it.

An easy argument involves computing a precision/recall-curve. Say you have a threshold that you can vary to output more or fewer predicted boundaries. If you move the threshold towards zero, you will output more and more boundaries, driving your recall towards 1.0 and your precision towards 0.0. If you move the threshold towards the maximum, you will output fewer and fewer boundaries, driving your precision towards 1.0 and your recall towards 0.0. Shortly before reaching the maximum, you will predict a single (assumedly correct) boundary, i.e., you have a precision of 1.0 and recall of 1.0/num_gt_boundaries. When you go a little higher, your (and sklearn's) method will suddenly drop the precision back to 0.0, while the limit says it should be 1.0.

Alright, after some setup I've managed to run Li Su's matlab code, and am able to produce eval results that are identical to the ones published in MIREX (with matlab). This confirms that the matlab code I have is the one that was used for MIREX 2015.

Next steps:

1. Turns out the Su dataset provides the ground truth in text format and also as MIDI files. The matlab code uses the MIDI files, whilst I was using the text files for mir_eval. So my first step is to confirm that the text gt files indeed match perfectly the MIDI gt files.

2. Assuming I confirm step (1), the next step will be to try and reproduce the matlab results using mir_eval. For this I will use the non-graph note matching python port of the matlab matching code, and try to figure out what Li Su's preprocessing is doing and imitate that too.

3. Assuming I manage to reproduce the mirex results in python, the next step will be to slowly get rid of the preprocessing in order to study how this affects the results:
3.a. Replace the note matching with the graph note matching, and see how things change
3.b. Remove the preprocessing, and see how things change
3.c. Document all of these changes here and in transcription.py

4. Upon inspection of the code, I realized Li Su and I have different interpretations of the verbal description of the metrics as published on the MIREX website:
> This subtask is evaluated in two different ways. In the first setup , a returned note is assumed correct if its onset is within +-50ms of a ref note and its F0 is within +- quarter tone of the corresponding reference note, ignoring the returned offset values. In the second setup, on top of the above requirements, a correct returned note is required to have an offset value within 20% of the ref notes duration around the ref note`s offset, or within 50ms whichever is larger.

The ambiguous point is about the offset matching. The way I understood it is that you compute a window that's 20% of the ref note's duration, and then you center this window on the ref offset, so the distance between the est offset and the ref offset can be at most half this window. According to Li Su's code, the allowed distance between the offsets is the full window.

To illustrate my point: let's say you have a reference note between times 2.0 and 3.0 (so the duration is 1 second). According to my parsing, we take 20% of this duration, i.e. 0.2 s, and center it on the offset. This means the beginning of this 200 ms window will be at time 2.9, and end at time 3.1. So if we take the absolute different between the offset times, it should be <= 0.1, which is half the window size. According to the matlab code, the calculation would allow the offset to be between 2.8 and 3.2 ms, i.e. it checks that the absolute distance between the offsets is <= 0.2.

We're in the process of contacting some "multi-f0 elders" to see if anyone can help resolve this ambiguity.

> Does it make sense to write a new method in io for this scenario (e.g. load_notes, load_transcription or load_realvalued_intervals)

Yes.  It should look very similar to `load_labeled_intervals`, except that it should call `load_delimited` with `[float, float, float]` as you suggested.  It should validate the resulting intervals, too, using `util.validate_intevals`.  I would call it something more generic than `load_notes` or `load_transcription`.  I like `load_valued_intervals`.

> Also, do we have any preference for handling corner cases: if either the reference or estimate are empty, then the precision, recall, and f-measure (or a subset of these) will be undefined due to a division by zero. Should the metrics return None, raise an error, or a hardcoded 0 or 1 in such cases?

It depends on the definition of the metric itself.  Given empty annotations, we always trigger a warning in the validate function.  In precision/recall/F-mesure-based metrics, we return 0s.

> I'll just add to the mix that I don't think it's reasonable for a user to memorize a mapping from 12 colors to 12 chords (even if the colors are easily distinguishable), even with a legend at hand.

Sure, but the explicit values are much less important than just being able to compare them.  The most important operation when looking at the graph will be "Are these two chords the same?" both when comparing two (ref/est) graphs and when looking at a single one.  I mean, you could say the exact same thing about vertical positioning of different chords - of course we don't expect people to memorize a mapping between height and N different chords either.  In this case, we just want them to be able to see that the chords are different (because they have different vertical heights), and they can refer to the y labels as needed.

@drybjed: The actual problem you're describing is not whether `acme-tiny` should support returning intermediate certificates, but what `acme-tiny` should do **by default**.

Just because there are situations in which other tools take care of downloading intermediate certificates, there's absolutely no reason why `acme-tiny` shouldn't be able to return intermediate certificates. Letting `acme-tiny` return intermediate certificates is - by far and away! - the easiest way to retrieve them. Just look at this PR, it adds just 7 LoC, external tools doing this reliably often need more LoC than `acme-tiny`.

Not to mention that `acme-tiny` by itself doesn't support auto-renewal of certificates at the moment (in contrast to what the `README.md` says)...

Thus, the only question here is whether `acme-tiny` should return intermediate certificates by default or not - not whether it should be able to do so. I agree with you that `acme-tiny` shouldn't do that. Not because I think that intermediate certificates usually aren't required (that's definitely not the case, it's the other way round), but because we shouldn't break BC. Many tools and scripts using `acme-tiny` rely on that it outputs the certificate only (e.g. `acme-tiny … > cert.pem`). Thus there should be a option flag to specifically instruct `acme-tiny` to append the intermediate certificate.

Considering @diafygi's position on adding a `--output` option, I think he would rather accept this with a boolean option flag to append the intermediate certificate to `stdout` (i.e. not a option receiving a path where the intermediate certificate should be stored).

To handle the issue of *any* column being modified by two different clients at the same time, I'm adding a check to verify that any old data in the entity is the same prior to writing. If any prior data has changed, you get a new `DataRaceError()` exception.

To check the deleted case, we do the exact same thing, only also add a check to ensure that the primary key is stored in the entity data. In the case that the entity does not exist, we raise a `EntityDeletedError()` exception, requiring that the user use `entity.save(force=True)` to re-save the entire entity.

```
coverage run --source=. -m py.test --cov=. --cov-branch --cov-report term-missing
================================== test session starts ==================================
platform linux -- Python 3.5.2, pytest-3.6.3, py-1.5.4, pluggy-0.6.0
rootdir: /home/antek/praca/evox/coverage_test, inifile:
plugins: cov-2.5.1
collected 4 items                                                                       

test_foo.py ....                                                                  [100%]

----------- coverage: platform linux, python 3.5.2-final-0 -----------
Name          Stmts   Miss Branch BrPart  Cover   Missing
---------------------------------------------------------
test_foo.py      12      0      6      2    89%   7->exit, 12->exit


=============================== 4 passed in 0.04 seconds ================================
Coverage.py warning: No data was collected. (no-data-collected)
```

I solved the mystery. The one to blame is pytest and it's "assertion rewrite hook". As far as I understand, it changes the code of all `assert`s to be a special fancy assert-like function that shows better errors. The problem is, that for some reason it breaks the coverage for cases like:
```
    assert (
        [x for x in range(3)] == [0, 1, 2]
    )
```
Quick test - let's pretend that our system does not support the hook: (`.../site-packages/_pytest/assertion/__init.__.py`) and always raise the Jython-dependent error:
```
def install_importhook(config):
    """Try to install the rewrite hook, raise SystemError if it fails."""
    raise SystemError("rewrite not supported") # <-- add this to force an Exception to not install the hook
    # Jython has an AST bug that make the assertion rewriting hook malfunction.
    if sys.platform.startswith("java"):
        raise SystemError("rewrite not supported")
```
And voilla, no branches uncovered.

So to sum up - the bug is somewhere inside the assertion rewrite mechanism of pytest. I will open an issue there with link to this issue for reference.

How old is the system sqlite3? `sqlite3 --version` should show you.

In `connection.c` we are checking the SQLite version for load extension availability, so as long as the version is reasonably recent it should be fine:

```c
#if SQLITE_VERSION_NUMBER >= 3003008
#ifndef SQLITE_OMIT_LOAD_EXTENSION
#define HAVE_LOAD_EXTENSION
#endif
#endif
```

You might also make sure that your `sqlite3` was compiled with `ENABLE_LOAD_EXTENSION` compile-time option set.

threads, two of which are fast as they just call len('eppy') or

are started fast, slow, fast and the results should come back fast, fast,
slow. But running the tiny test IDF is also pretty fast so sometimes they
come back out of order. As I say, if I make the run period longer in the
IDF it should make the test more reliable.

When you start a stream, the first event you will received is the current state of the tree relative to the subscribed path. This is the expected functionality. Further events will reflect changes in that subtree.

I guess you could subscribe to the whole tree so you get changes that happen all over the database. Keep in mind that if the database is big, then you will start to experience delays when downloading the whole tree at first.

Pyrebase is, in essence, a wrapper around Firebase Rest API. So you can check the Firebase docs to see what's possible and what's not from their docs too. The relevant section for streaming is: https://firebase.google.com/docs/reference/rest/database/#section-streaming

I think this is really hard (or impossible) to get right, especially across multiple platforms.
If it were easier, Erik would have done it in libsndfile.

If we do this, we should at least keep the original error message as part of the new error message, because I'm quite sure there will be some cases where our new error message will actually be worse than libsndfile's.

We can try to check if a file exists with `os.path.isfile()`, but this would only work on a subset of cases.
We would have to handle named pipes separately, since they would return `False` since they are not "normal" files.
OTOH, there are cases where `isfile()` returns `True`, but libsndfile will still not be able to open the file, e.g. if the user doesn't have the necessary access rights.

So we would have to add a lot of code, just to cover a subset of the error cases; and in the end we wouldn't even be sure if we got even those cases right.

In the last few months I often found myself writing elaborate exception messages explaining in detail every bit the user did wrong.
Then I saw that this is crazy, because all of a sudden all the documentation was inside of some exception messages instead of where it belongs: in the docstring!

Wouldn't it also in this case be better to mention in the docstring that a non-existing file (or one with in-appropriate access rights) might cause a "RuntimeError: System Error" and be done with it?

Perhaps a one character change to fulfill this request is to use `pool.imap` instead of `pool.map` here https://github.com/mementum/backtrader/blob/e52bc0a8/backtrader/cerebro.py#L130

That way, `runstrats` becomes a lazy iterable. There are 2 benefits:

- I can keep writing to an output file without waiting for the whole thing to finish. Surprisingly, many things can go wrong during optimization and what often happens is _one little bug_ discards hours of optimization result. This prevents it:

```python
for strat in cerebro.runstrats:
    write_result(strat[0].analyzers...)
```

- Not all the results need to be saved in memory, unless the end user requests it like `list(cerebro.runstrats)`

- I can easily wrap a progress bar around it. For example using [tqdm](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/tqdm)

```python
# I need to calculate `total` myself here. So maybe some utils can be devised to do that for me as well. But still better than a black screen of nothing
for strat in tqdm.tqdm(cerebro.runstrats, total=1000):
    print_analysis(strat)
```

You may try the followind code for data feed provided by a "Data" from blaze.

According to the blaze documents chunking is automatically activated if the data source is large, so there should be no need to pass the chunks to a Pandas DataFrame.

To use the data feed below:

````python

blaze_data = Data('mycsv.csv')  # or a database uri
data = BlazeData(dataname=blaze_data)

cerebro = backtrader.Cerebro()
cerebro.adddata(data)

cerebro.addstrategy(MyStrategy)

cerebro.run()
````

In any case: the entire dataset will be loaded in memory) and the code is untested and may still contain typos

````python
class BlazeData(feed.DataBase):
    '''
    The ``dataname`` parameter inherited from ``feed.DataBase`` is the blaze
    Data
    '''

    params = (
        # datetime must be present
        ('datetime', 0),
        # pass -1 for any of the following to indicate absence
        ('open', 1),
        ('high', 2),
        ('low', 3),
        ('close', 4),
        ('volume', 5),
        ('openinterest', 6),
    )

    datafields = [
        'datetime', 'open', 'high', 'low', 'close', 'volume', 'openinterest'
    ]

    def start(self):
        # reset the iterator on each start
        self._rows = iter(self.p.dataname)

    def _load(self):
        try:
            row = next(self._rows)
        except StopIteration:
            return False

        # Set the standard datafields - except for datetime
        for datafield in self.datafields[1:]:
            # get the column index
            colidx = getattr(self.params, datafield)

            if colidx < 0:
                # column not present -- skip
                continue

            # get the line to be set
            line = getattr(self.lines, datafield)
            line[0] = row[colidx]

        # datetime - assumed blaze always serves a native datetime.datetime
        colidx = getattr(self.params, self.datafields[0])
        dt = row[colidx]
        dtnum = date2num(dt)

        # get the line to be set
        line = getattr(self.lines, self.datafields[0])
        line[0] = dtnum

        # Done ... return
        return True
````

request can be safely imported and used from the flask package itself. This is how it's actually intended to be used.

So, if you had a requirement like:

    def can_access_post(user, request):
        post_id = request.view_args.get('post_id')
        # other things

You can continue using that requirement for now. However you'll get a warning when it's invoked (hopefully only the first time it's invoked, I'm nervously awaiting the big report that I've flooded someone's logs with warnings). To stop the warning, the change to make is:

    from flask import request 
    
    def can_access_post(user):
        post_id = request.view_args.get('post_id')
        # other things

Which is almost identical, with the difference being you're receiving the request object from a different location. 

The reasons for deprecating this argument are several:

1. It was a concession made strictly for testing. Typically if tests are easy to write, I think I have a good API but it turns out they lied to me this time. 🤷‍♂️

2. The request is already accessible to the requirements anyways as evidenced above. 

3. The way Allows passes the request is potentially dangerous depending on how its used in the requirements itself. It passes the context local rather than the actual request itself. If someone wrote a requirement that somehow passed that requirement into a different thread (or greenlet or similar), then that's a bug but that's on me for not warning about that.

Point 3 I didn't realize until earlier today and didn't factor into my decision, but it's honestly a better reason for ditching it. 

In any case, I'm not planning on fully removing support until the mythical 1.0 release which should give people plenty of time to migrate away from using it.

My thinking is the following:

In floyd 0.8 the only place the ‘0’ can possibly come from is the info strings written during search. The search is running in a child process different from the uci parsing main process.
Normally these info strings are followed by a flush of the output buffer provided by the C library. However there is one condition where the flush is postponed, and that is in the first 0.1 seconds of search.

In any case, the end of a search is indicated with a 'bestmove’ line, which is always followed by a flush in floyd. So even info strings written in the first 0.1 seconds will be flushed. After ‘bestmove’, floyd won’t write anything unless asked for.

So from my point of view (and with the limited information I have), this ‘0readyok’ can happen when the uci-host (python-chess) sends a ‘isready’ command to the engine while it is thinking. In this case very quickly after starting a search.

If that is indeed the case, I believe python-chess is at fault, as when an UCI engine is thinking the UCI host can only wait for the move to complete, or stop the search with ‘stop’ before sending anything else. (The only other valid command is ‘ponderhit’, but after that it still can’t send ‘isready’)

Full logging needs to confirm, but I suspect floyd is ok here.

If lfoyd is in the wrong, I have to consider fixes or workarounds:
1. I could add extra flush commands in the first 0.1 seconds, but that would cost a bit of elo in very fast games.
2. Another workaround could be to add an extra newline before ‘readyok’. But there is clearly some garbage in the output buffer.
3. A third possibility is to let any unexpected command stop the search, or at least ‘isready’.
I’m hesitant to proceed with any of the three until I better understand the issue.

Marcel

It's because you can't have a function that mixes the order of arguments with and without defaults.  No matter where you put a single dividing line you will be able to define either positional-or-keyword arguments with and without defaults and keyword-only arguments with defaults, or positional-or-keyword arguments without defaults and keyword-only arguments with and without defaults, never all four.  Example: `f(a, b, c, d=0, e=1)`.  If you divide after b, you'll get c, d=0, and e=1 as keyword-only arguments, and a and b as positional-or-keyword arguments without defaults.  If you divide after d, you'll get e=1 as a keyword-only argument with a default, and a, b, c, and d=0 as positional-or-keyword arguments.

Hi,
we've written a simple class `MultiTrigger` which suffices for our use cases.
I'll gladly share its code and some tests.
Perhaps this helps.

We use it mainly to have jobs with multiple cron triggers.
Please note that usually a trigger is informed about the previous fire time. Here, we translated this to "the trigger's" previous fire time as opposed to "the job's" previous run time.
In my opinion, this is the main trade off: Some triggers might rely on the previous fire being that of the trigger (like the builtin triggers: `IntervalTrigger`'s `next_fire_time = previous_fire_time + self.interval` and `DateTrigger`'s `self.run_date if previous_fire_time is None else None` and `CronTrigger`), while custom triggers might interpret it as the last triggered run time of the job.
We targeted compatibility with the builtin triggers.

Comments are welcome :)

```
from __future__ import division, print_function

from operator import itemgetter
from itertools import ifilter, izip, chain

from apscheduler.triggers.base import BaseTrigger


__all__ = [
    'MultiTrigger',
]


class MultiTrigger(BaseTrigger):

    def __init__(self, *triggers):
        """
        I am a trigger container to allow for single jobs to have more complex triggers.
        Given a set of triggers, the next fire time is set as the minimum over the next fire times of the triggers.
        Roughly meaning: min(t.get_next_fire_time(prev, now) for t in triggers) where each individual trigger gets
        its own previous runtime. (Note however, that this is not guaranteed. Triggers that depend on a previous fire
        time might be problematic.)

        Construct via MultiTrigger(<iterable of BaseTriggers>) or MultiTrigger(<BaseTrigger>, ...) or mix the two.

        Parameters
        ----------
        triggers : apscheduler.triggers.base.BaseTriggers or iterables of apscheduler.triggers.base.BaseTriggers


        Examples
        --------
        >>> import os
        >>> os.environ['TZ'] = 'UTC'
        >>> from pytz import utc
        >>> from datetime import datetime, timedelta
        >>> from apscheduler.triggers.interval import IntervalTrigger
        >>> from apscheduler.triggers.date import DateTrigger
        >>> from apscheduler.triggers.cron import CronTrigger
        >>> m = MultiTrigger(IntervalTrigger(hours=10, start_date=datetime(2015, 7, 7), end_date=datetime(2015, 7, 8)),
        ...                  CronTrigger(hour='14,17', timezone='CET'),
        ...                  CronTrigger(hour=15, minute=30, timezone='CET'),
        ...                  DateTrigger(datetime(2015, 7, 7, 22, 42, 42, 42)))
        >>> t = utc.localize(datetime(2015, 7, 6))
        >>> while t < utc.localize(datetime(2015, 7, 9)):
        ...     t = m.get_next_fire_time(t, t + timedelta(minutes=1))
        ...     t
        datetime.datetime(2015, 7, 6, 14, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'CET' CEST+2:00:00 DST>)
        datetime.datetime(2015, 7, 6, 15, 30, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'CET' CEST+2:00:00 DST>)
        datetime.datetime(2015, 7, 6, 17, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'CET' CEST+2:00:00 DST>)
        datetime.datetime(2015, 7, 7, 0, 0, tzinfo=<UTC>)
        datetime.datetime(2015, 7, 7, 10, 0, tzinfo=<UTC>)
        datetime.datetime(2015, 7, 7, 14, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'CET' CEST+2:00:00 DST>)
        datetime.datetime(2015, 7, 7, 15, 30, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'CET' CEST+2:00:00 DST>)
        datetime.datetime(2015, 7, 7, 17, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'CET' CEST+2:00:00 DST>)
        datetime.datetime(2015, 7, 7, 20, 0, tzinfo=<UTC>)
        datetime.datetime(2015, 7, 7, 22, 42, 42, 42, tzinfo=<UTC>)
        datetime.datetime(2015, 7, 8, 14, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'CET' CEST+2:00:00 DST>)
        datetime.datetime(2015, 7, 8, 15, 30, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'CET' CEST+2:00:00 DST>)
        datetime.datetime(2015, 7, 8, 17, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'CET' CEST+2:00:00 DST>)
        datetime.datetime(2015, 7, 9, 14, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'CET' CEST+2:00:00 DST>)
        """
        self.triggers = list(chain((t for t in triggers if isinstance(t, BaseTrigger)),
                                   chain.from_iterable(t for t in triggers if not isinstance(t, BaseTrigger))))

        assert self.triggers, 'triggers must be non-empty'
        for i, t in enumerate(self.triggers):
            if not isinstance(t, BaseTrigger):
                raise ValueError('object no %d is not of type BaseTrigger but %s: %s' % (i, type(t), t))

        self.previous_fire_times = [None] * len(self.triggers)
        self.previous_trigger_idx = None

        start_dates = [getattr(trigger, 'start_date', None) for trigger in self.triggers]
        end_dates = [getattr(trigger, 'end_date', None) for trigger in self.triggers]
        self.start_date = None if not start_dates or None in start_dates else min(start_dates)
        self.end_date = None if not end_dates or None in end_dates else max(end_dates)

    def get_next_fire_time(self, previous_fire_time, now):
        if previous_fire_time is not None and self.previous_trigger_idx is not None:
            self.previous_fire_times[self.previous_trigger_idx] = previous_fire_time

        next_fire_time, self.previous_trigger_idx = min(
            ifilter(
                itemgetter(0),  # filter out Nones
                ((t.get_next_fire_time(p, now), i)
                 for i, (t, p) in enumerate(izip(self.triggers, self.previous_fire_times)))
            )
        )

        return next_fire_time

    def __str__(self):
        return 'multi[%s]' % ', '.join(str(x) for x in self.triggers)

    def __repr__(self):
        return "<%s %s>" % (self.__class__.__name__, repr(self.triggers))
```

```
import pytz
from apscheduler.triggers.cron import CronTrigger

from mypackage import MultiTrigger


class TestMultiTrigger(object):
    def test_str(self):
        timezone = pytz.timezone('Europe/Berlin')
        m = MultiTrigger(
            CronTrigger(hour='14,17', timezone=timezone),
            CronTrigger(hour=15, minute=30, timezone=timezone),
            CronTrigger(month=7, day='1st sun', hour=13, minute=0, second=30, timezone='UTC'),
        )
        assert str(m) == "multi[" \
                             "cron[hour='14,17'], " \
                             "cron[hour='15', minute='30'], " \
                             "cron[month='7', day='1st sun', hour='13', minute='0', second='30']" \
                         "]"

    def test_fire_times(self):
        timezone = pytz.timezone('Europe/Berlin')
        expected_fire_times = [
            timezone.localize(datetime(2015, 7, 4, 14, 0)),
            timezone.localize(datetime(2015, 7, 4, 15, 30)),
            timezone.localize(datetime(2015, 7, 4, 17, 0)),
            timezone.localize(datetime(2015, 7, 5, 14, 0)),
            pytz.utc.localize(datetime(2015, 7, 5, 13, 0, 30)),  # that's the first sunday!
            timezone.localize(datetime(2015, 7, 5, 15, 30)),
            timezone.localize(datetime(2015, 7, 5, 17, 0)),
            timezone.localize(datetime(2015, 7, 6, 14, 0)),
            timezone.localize(datetime(2015, 7, 6, 15, 30)),
            timezone.localize(datetime(2015, 7, 6, 17, 0)),
            timezone.localize(datetime(2015, 7, 7, 14, 0)),
            timezone.localize(datetime(2015, 7, 7, 15, 30)),
            timezone.localize(datetime(2015, 7, 7, 17, 0)),
            timezone.localize(datetime(2015, 7, 8, 14, 0)),
            timezone.localize(datetime(2015, 7, 8, 15, 30)),
            timezone.localize(datetime(2015, 7, 8, 17, 0)),
        ]

        m = MultiTrigger(
            CronTrigger(hour='14,17', timezone=timezone),
            CronTrigger(hour=15, minute=30, timezone=timezone),
            CronTrigger(month=7, day='1st sun', hour=13, minute=0, second=30, timezone='UTC'),
        )
        last_fire_time = pytz.utc.localize(datetime(2015, 7, 4))
        for expected_fire_time in expected_fire_times:
            fire_time = m.get_next_fire_time(last_fire_time, last_fire_time + timedelta(minutes=1))
            assert str(fire_time) == str(expected_fire_time)
            last_fire_time = fire_time
```

Thanks for your feedback! The problem is that there are two assumptions I could choose from when writing this program:

1. Always encoding losslessly to prevent generation loss
2. Always encoding with the best possible encoder for the output format

Due to the technical background of this script, I've opted for the first option. Also, I cannot reasonably automatically identify if a given output extension supports PCM encoding or not.

In the latter case I could actually have ffmpeg select the encoder itself – if you don't explicitly specify PCM WAV, it'll normally just auto-pick whatever the format (e.g. `mp3`) requires.

I think the best solution would be to auto-detect impossible combinations (like PCM audio in MP3 containers) and print an error, telling the user to explicitly specify the encoder.

I find it sad that the first issue in an open sourced software project is related to... "legalities" and not... software. I'm not a lawyer and this is not legal advice. I just like to study this topic and I'd like to contribute with my ideas, just for a critical reflection about this controversial topic.

# Short story

Google doesn't own exclusive rights over "automated data gathering" processes and they can't prohibit others to do it. Remember: they do it themselves thousands of times every single second. I could have a ToS stating that once Google indexes my website, they enter into a contract and give me the right to index their website as well.

Google Page Rank is protected by database copyright and it's a fair protection. I cannot copy the ordering of pages and create another search service using it. But in most countries, I can use the search results to create another service, something innovative, and I'm not infringing copyright. Google's willingness to restrict other people's right to innovate (that they benefit themselves from, using other people's content) is outrageous.

# Long Story

### Be careful about considering ToS blindly as law

Considering a website Terms of Service (ToS) blindly as "law" may lead to outcomes that one could reasonably argue as undesirable. Take this example: [Internet Archive v. Suzanne Shell](http://www.internetlibrary.com/cases/lib_case456.cfm). Suzanne put a notice in her website ToS stating that, once you copy data from her website, you enter into a contract and you owe her US$5,000 per web page copied. Yes, this is really true, this lawsuit really happened.

### If ToS is law, I can have a ToS giving me the same rights as Google gives itself to scrape the web

If I would really think that anything written in a ToS is enforceable and I still wanted to scrape Google, I'd just put a ToS on my website stating something like: if you scrape any content from our website, you automatically give us an irrevocable equal right to also scrape any content from your website(s). Since I blindly treat ToS as "law", once Google indexes my website I would have the right to scrape Google's website as well.

### Google doesn't have exclusive rights over "automated data gathering"

Honestly, it is an outright hypocrisy for Google to prohibit "automated data gathering". The entire Google business model is based on automated data gathering and processing. Google DO NOT own automated data gathering processes, they DO NOT have any exclusive rights over this. They didn't even create it.

### Two cents about database copyrights

Removing the discussion about "manually is ok, automated is not ok", it all comes to the database itself. The question is: does Google hold copyright over the content they serve on their Search service? The answer is: it depends on the country.

In the UK and the EU, there's the old-fashioned "[Sui Generis Database Right](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sui_generis_database_right)". I heard no court has ever enforced it in Europe, but, in theory, the answer is yes: Google owns copyright over the list of web pages they serve on their Search service and you cannot use it (either gathering automatically or manually) without their prior consent.

Most other world regions (including most countries, if not all, in North, Central, and South America) adopted a different approach that is more friendly to innovation. It's based on the [Berne Convention](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berne_Convention). In summary, a database owner only has rights over the ordering of the database (if there's a creative work in the ordering), not the content itself (unless the content is a creative work, which is not the case for Google). For example, Google worked very hard to come up with a way to order web pages, what is called "Page Rank". The approach proposed by the Berne Convention is that Google owns the ordering, but not the content that is ordered, which I think is fair.

Google's business model would be dead if they needed to respect the "Sui Generis Database" rights. Google is one of the most benefited from the innovation-friendly ideas conveyed by the Berne Convention. Google should be the first one advocating to **protect**, not **restrict**, everyone's rights to use non-creative works in innovative ways like they did. **Google ToS is actually infringing other people's rights to innovate, IMHO.**

### I cannot copy Google's ordering (Page Rank), but I can derive innovative works from Search Results

Now, under the light of the Berne Convention, if I scrape Google and create another search service following the same ordering of pages, I would be offending a copyright. And it's fair because Google invested so much time and money on their Page Rank and this must be protected. On the other hand, if I scrape Google and use the content to do something else, combine with other data, create something different, new, innovative, then I'm not offending any copyrights.

Ok I updated the string escaping to be pretty robust.

I added the `drawtext` filter since its `text` arg has slightly more complicated string escaping that other filter args.

The string escaping actually makes sense to me now. The reason why there are so many backslashes is that `\` and `'` characters are escape/unescaped numerous times as the command-line is constructed/destructed. It's actually intentional by the ffmpeg designers so that tools like `ffmpeg-python` can programmatically escape things in a well-defined manner. It's a pain to do by hand, but libraries like this can automate it and make it a non-issue.

The tests are table-driven to make sure all of the characters are escaped as desired, both in the `drawtext` `text` arg and normal args. This is nearly a direct translation of the science table I posted earlier with the character -> expected backslash count:
```
    expected_backslash_counts = {
        'x': 0,
        '\'': 7,
        '\\': 7,
        '%': 4,
        ':': 2,
        ',': 1,
        '[': 1,
        ']': 1,
        '=': 2,
        '\n': 0,
    }
    for ch, expected_backslash_count in expected_backslash_counts.items():
        expected = '{}{}'.format('\\' * expected_backslash_count, ch)
        actual = _get_drawtext_text_repr(ch)
        assert expected == actual
```
So basically with these tests, we should be pretty confident that we escape all things correctly in all circumstances. And if there are any extra edge cases, we can just add it to the above test table and then see the tests pass once we fix the code.

```
import codecs, json
```

```python
# my_project/fields
from multiupload.fields import MultiImageField

class ValidatedMultiImageField(MultiImageField):
    def run_validators(self, value):
        value = value or []

        for item in value:
            super().run_validators(item)
```

```python

class FooModelForm(forms.ModelForm):
    attachments = ValidatedMultiImageField()
```

I've pushed out a new release so you can now install progressbar2 3.16.1 :)

I've added support for a `custom_len` function to the progressbar. This makes it possible to specify your own custom length function, it seems the `wcwidth` library doesn't support these characters either so I've added an example to the readme to show at least one workaround.

```python
# vim: fileencoding=utf-8
import time
import progressbar


def custom_len(value):
    # These characters take up more space
    characters = {
        '进': 2,
        '度': 2,
    }

    total = 0
    for c in value:
        total += characters.get(c, 1)

    return total


bar = progressbar.ProgressBar(
    widgets=[
        '进度: ',
        progressbar.Bar(),
        ' ',
        progressbar.Counter(format='%(value)02d/%(max_value)d'),
    ],
    len_func=custom_len,
)
for i in bar(range(10)):
    time.sleep(0.1)
```

The relevant log lines are:

homegear.err
```
04/28/17 11:23:05.086 Module HomeMatic BidCoS: HM-MOD-RPI-PCB "My-HM-MOD-RPI-PCB": Warning: AES handshake failed for packet: 0D3EA6104DEEB9FD4A7D06010000F2
Removing server "homeassistant-wireless". Server has to send "init" again.
```

homegear.log
```
04/28/17 11:23:05.085 Module HomeMatic BidCoS: HM-MOD-RPI-PCB "My-HM-MOD-RPI-PCB": Warning: AES handshake failed for packet: 0D3EA6104DEEB9FD4A7D06010000F2
04/28/17 11:23:05.086 RPC client: Info: Calling RPC method "error" on server 192.168.1.23.
```

What happens is, that Homegear drops the connection because of the error with the failed AES handshake. This is outside of the scope of HASS and / or pyhomematic.

You can use the [homematic.reconnect](https://home-assistant.io/components/homematic/#services) service to make HASS talk to Homegear again. If you have something like a brightness sensor that regularily reports the brightness, you could use an automation that executes the reconnect service as soon as that brightness-sensor has the same value for one hour.
Another approach would be to have some script that regularily looks at the homegear.err file to check id the same error has occured again, and if yes, reconnect.

To get this issue resolved permanently without any workaround, you probably have to create an issue over at the [Homegear](https://github.com/Homegear/Homegear) repo. pyhomematic is just a "man in the middle" between HASS and Homegear, and checking if the connection is still alive is not part of the tasks pyhomematic should take care of. And since neither the CCU nor Homegear tells pyhomematic that the connection has been dropped, there's no clean way to handle this besides simple re-connecting.

Well, `PYTHONIOENCODING` generally dictates the encoding of `sys.std*` objects, which may be usefull even in Windows. AFAIK when you redirect the content of a text file to Python the encoding of the file should match the encoding of `sys.stdin` and if you encode you plaintext files in UTF-8, using `PYTHONIOENCODING` is a way to tell that to Python in Windows.

I wouldn't worry too much about deprecation policies, other than modifying your [setup.py](https://github.com/alanjds/drf-nested-routers/blob/master/setup.py#L90-L94) with the appropriate library versions and making sure that minor releases do not remove any support for the given dependencies.
(This means that major releases should be allowed to break compatibility with "older" versions of some dependencies)

Also, I'm not 100% sure about this (somebody can surely confirm or deny this), but you can specify framework versions in your setup.py, e.g.:
```
        'Framework :: Django',
        'Framework :: Django :: 1.8',
        'Framework :: Django :: 1.9',
        'Framework :: Django :: 1.10',
```
Despite this you can always specify dependencies (as you already did) and make sure nobody installs a "wrong" version of your component:
```
    install_requires=[
        'djangorestframework>=2.4.3',
        'Django>=1.6',
    ],
```

Some 95% of Crontab's purpose was to be the cron scheduling library for my other project, Rpqueue. Rpqueue doesn't do timezones, so I didn't build Crontab to do timezones either. Not to say I can't (turns out it's really easy to add), I'm just explaining why it is the way it is.

That said, I get the same result as you when I run your code, but your code is partly wrong:
```
>>> datetime(2016, 3, 13, 5, tzinfo=eastern)
datetime.datetime(2016, 3, 13, 5, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'US/Eastern' LMT-1 day, 19:04:00 STD>)
```

Compare that to the below from the pytz docs:
```
>>> datetime(2016, 3, 13, 5, tzinfo=pytz.utc).astimezone(eastern)
datetime.datetime(2016, 3, 13, 0, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'US/Eastern' EST-1 day, 19:00:00 STD>)
```

With the "correct" datetime, I get 9 hours instead of 4:
```
>>> dt = datetime(2016, 3, 13, 5, tzinfo=pytz.utc).astimezone(eastern)
>>> crontab.CronTab('0 9 13 3 *').next(dt)
32400.0
```

That's not terribly helpful, because the actual timing should be 8 hours due to the DST change, but it turns out that the fix is easy:

```
>>> def next_with_timezone(ct, now):
...     fixed = now.replace(tzinfo=None)
...     runtime = datetime.utcfromtimestamp(ct.next(fixed, delta=False))
...     return now.tzinfo.localize(runtime) - now
... 
>>> next_with_timezone(crontab.CronTab('0 9 13 3 *'), dt)
datetime.timedelta(0, 28800)
```

That function will get you going for now, and I'll add a method that does the same soon-ish.

Indeed, drawing a Venn diagram with ellipses instead of circles would increase its degrees of freedom and make it possible to avoid such situations. However, designing an algorithm for reasonably pretty ellipse-venn positioning and subsequent clipping is an endeavor worthy of a separate project for which I myself do not have the time or motivation at the moment. In fact, before attempting this, one should develop a general Bezier-shape intersection library for Python, it is still missing and without it implementing Ellipse-Ellipse intersection would be a pain.

The best what one may probably expect to do to solve the "missing region" problem for circle-based Venn diagrams is to come up with a way to position the *label* of such a region even though the region itself would be missing or empty. Unfortunately, it is also not trivial. In the current solution the geometric shape of each region (computed as the appropriate intersection of circles) is used as the basis for finding a proper label position within that shape. But if there is no shape, there is no place to put the label and some ad-hoc techniques should be applied in order to "invent" the label position out of nothing.

I am keeping this issue open to collect particular examples of such corner cases. Once there are enough of them I (or why not someone else) might look into adding some ad-hoc tricks for preserving the labels of the "disappeared" regions, which would solve at least the known problem samples.

Before that happens, you should resort to manual hacks. At least two are possible:
 
 * You can simply add the missing label manually (e.g. via the `annotate`, or `text` command, guessing the appropriate coordinates by trial and error).
 * You can draw the diagram using tweaked numbers, such that the necessary region is not missing, and then manually change the text in the corresponding label to the correct number.
Suppose your current subset sizes are (k,l,m,n,o,p,q), and suppose that the subregion "AB not C" (with size m) is not shown on the diagram due to bad skew in favor of other regions. If you artificially increase the size of that region (from m to, say, m+100 or m+1000) chances are at some point it will appear on the diagram. You can then change the label of that region to the correct value. I.e.:

    ```python
     v = venn3((k,l,m + 100,n,o,p,q))
     v.get_label_by_id('110').set_text(m)
     ```

I'm going to have to set up custom Lookup classes for the different kinds of possible lookups that I want to support, and check for integer lookups there, so that `get_prep_value` can take either an int or a string. ```python
```python
class ServicesNamespace(RockstorIO):

    start = False

    def on_connect(self, sid, environ):

        self.emit('connected', {
            'key': 'services:connected', 'data': 'connected'
        })
        self.start = True
        self.spawn(self.send_service_statuses, sid) # <- use newly created spawn

    def on_disconnect(self, sid):

        self.cleanup(sid) # <- grant killing all "zombies"
        self.start = False
```

	 refresh_token = token['refresh_token']

Thanks for raising this.  Yes, I would expect the behaviour that you describe above too (i.e. for the numbering to start again from zero for test 2).

This is caused by having  `index` as a class attribute with `print_item` as a `classmethod`:

```python
# approvaltests/list_utils.py
class FormatLineItem(object):
    index = 0

    @classmethod
    def print_item(cls, x):
        text = str(cls.index) + ') ' + str(x)
        cls.index += 1
        return text
```
I think the bug could be fixed by using a standard method on the class instance along with `index` as an attribute on the instance, e.g.

```python
class FormatLineItem(object):
    def __init__(self):
        self.index = 0

    def print_item(self, x):
        text = str(self.index) + ') ' + str(x)
        self.index += 1
        return text
```

```python
    ratios = (0.5, 1, 2)
    rpn_pre_nms_top_n = 12000
    rpn_post_nms_top_n = 2000
    threshold = 0.7
    rpn_min_size = 16

    feat_len = 14
    H, W = feat_len, feat_len
    num_anchors = len(scales) * len(ratios)
    count_anchors = H * W * num_anchors

    def get_new_data(batch_size, ctx):
        '''
        cls_prob: (batch_size, 2 * num_anchors, H, W)
        bbox_pred: (batch_size, 4 * num_anchors, H, W)
        im_info: (batch_size, 3)
        '''

        cls_prob = mx.nd.empty((batch_size, 2 * num_anchors, H, W), dtype = np.float32, ctx = ctx)
        bbox_pred = mx.nd.empty((batch_size, 4 * num_anchors, H, W), dtype = np.float32, ctx = ctx)
        im_info = mx.nd.empty((batch_size, 3), dtype = np.float32, ctx = ctx)

        cls_prob = mx.nd.array(np.random.random(cls_prob.shape), ctx = ctx)
        bbox_pred = mx.nd.array(np.random.random(bbox_pred.shape), ctx = ctx)

        for i in range(batch_size):
            im_size = np.random.randint(100, feat_len * feature_stride, size = (2,))
            im_scale = np.random.randint(70, 100) / 100.0
            im_info[i, :] = [im_size[0], im_size[1], im_scale]
        return cls_prob, bbox_pred, im_info

    def check_proposal_consistency(op, batch_size):
        '''
            op is mx.nd.contrib.Proposal or mx.nd.contrib.MultiProposal
        '''
        cls_prob, bbox_pred, im_info = get_new_data(batch_size, mx.cpu(0))
        rois_cpu, score_cpu = op(
                cls_score = cls_prob,
                bbox_pred = bbox_pred,
                im_info = im_info,
                feature_stride = feature_stride,
                scales = scales,
                ratios = ratios,
                rpn_pre_nms_top_n = rpn_pre_nms_top_n,
                rpn_post_nms_top_n = rpn_post_nms_top_n,
                threshold = threshold,
                rpn_min_size = rpn_min_size, output_score = True)

        gpu_ctx = mx.gpu(0)

        # copy data to gpu from cpu
        cls_prob_gpu = cls_prob.as_in_context(gpu_ctx)
        bbox_pred_gpu = bbox_pred.as_in_context(gpu_ctx)
        im_info_gpu = im_info.as_in_context(gpu_ctx)

        rois_gpu, score_gpu = op(
                cls_score = cls_prob_gpu,
                bbox_pred = bbox_pred_gpu,
                im_info = im_info_gpu,
                feature_stride = feature_stride,
                scales = scales,
                ratios = ratios,
                rpn_pre_nms_top_n = rpn_pre_nms_top_n,
                rpn_post_nms_top_n = rpn_post_nms_top_n,
                threshold = threshold,
                rpn_min_size = rpn_min_size, output_score = True)

        print (rois_cpu.asnumpy(), rois_gpu.asnumpy())
        assert np.allclose(rois_cpu.asnumpy(), rois_gpu.asnumpy())
        assert np.allclose(score_cpu.asnumpy(), score_gpu.asnumpy())

    check_proposal_consistency(mx.nd.contrib.Proposal, 1)
    check_proposal_consistency(mx.nd.contrib.MultiProposal, 20)

test_multi_proposal_op()
print ("test ok")
```

```python
import time

class Broadcaster(object):
    @classmethod
    def make_pub(cls, ctx):
        socket = ctx.socket(zmq.PUB)
        socket.connect(ADDRESS_IN)
        return socket

    @classmethod
    def broadcast_without_copy(cls, ctx):
        socket = cls.make_pub(ctx)
        socket.send_multipart([b'broadcast', b'no copy'], copy=False)

    @classmethod
    def broadcast_with_copy(cls, ctx):
        socket = cls.make_pub(ctx)
        socket.send_multipart([b'broadcast', b'copy'], copy=True)


def listen():
    ctx = zmq.Context()
    sub = ctx.socket(zmq.SUB)
    sub.setsockopt(zmq.SUBSCRIBE, b'')
    sub.connect(ADDRESS_OUT)
    while True:
        if sub.poll(timeout=1000):
            topic, msg = sub.recv_multipart()
            print(topic, msg)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    proxy = ThreadProxy(zmq.SUB, zmq.PUB)
    proxy.bind_in(ADDRESS_IN)
    proxy.bind_out(ADDRESS_OUT)
    proxy.setsockopt_in(zmq.SUBSCRIBE, b'')
    proxy.start()

    thread = Thread(target=listen)
    thread.start()

    time.sleep(1)

    ctx = zmq.Context()
    Broadcaster.broadcast_without_copy(ctx)
    Broadcaster.broadcast_with_copy(ctx)
```

```
def shorten(uri):
    query_params = {
        'access_token': settings.BITLY_OAUTH2_TOKEN,
        'longUrl': uri
    }

    endpoint = 'https://api-ssl.bitly.com/v3/shorten'
    response = requests.get(endpoint, params=query_params, verify=False)

    data = response.json()


    if not data['status_code'] == 200:
        logger.error("Unexpected status_code: {} in bitly response. {}".format(data['status_code'], response.text))

    return data['data']['url']
```

@jamesls your understanding of the issue is correct. urllib3 maintains a cache of idle TCP connections (the "pool"), but it's set at a fixed size. If there are no connections available from the pool, it creates a new one. When it's finished with that new connection it tries to return it to the pool, but if the pool is already full then it simply discards it. urllib3 issues a logging warning when it does so, since this essentially represents an inefficiency where the pool size is smaller than the number of simultaneous requests.

Both urllib3 and the Python requests library that wraps it expose settings to change the pool size, but because the connections are created under the hood by boto we don't have access to these settings from the boto client (without monkeypatching or similar).

Plumbing through the `pool_connections` setting that the requests HTTPAdapter exposes (as mentioned in the original post on this issue) would allow us to set the pool size to whatever is appropriate for our load. It should also be easy to do, it's simply exposing a parameter and then passing that parameter value to the underlying requests library. A more general solution would be to expose overrides for all the default HTTPAdapter settings, but this is the only one I really care about.

I'll note that although this issue is probably worst for services like DynamoDB in our case we trigger the error when loading more than 10 photos from S3 simultaneously, so hopefully whatever solution you arrive at is not specific to DynamoDB.

Lastly, simply for reference if you're curious, here's the place in the urllib3 connection pool code where the warning is issued:

https://github.com/shazow/urllib3/blob/65b8c52c16dee5c3a523de2c1c21853ba0e581f2/urllib3/connectionpool.py#L257

and here's the docs for the connection pool:

https://urllib3.readthedocs.io/en/1.4/pools.html

and here's a sort of how-to article on the setting in the requests library with way more information than you probably want on the subject:

https://laike9m.com/blog/requests-secret-pool_connections-and-pool_maxsize,89/

this is intended behavior

While I *can* treat things as editable packages, it really doesn't make much conceptual sense to do so, as none of the charger software is intended for publication in any of the Python-specific package formats, I just need to be able to specify a locked set of Python dependencies to be downloaded, bundled, and then installed into the on-device virtual environment.

That said, reading a `locked-requirements.txt` in the dummy `setup.py` I already created for `wagon`'s benefit [1] *would* be a significantly better near-term workaround than adding a dependency snapshot directly to `Pipfile` (thank you for the suggestion, @uranusjr!).

It's still not a complete solution though, since `pipenv` commands won't automatically detect that the lock file is out of date when `locked-requirements.txt` changes - I'll also need to add an explicit lock file regeneration step into the script that collects and locks the charger level dependencies.

[1] https://github.com/cloudify-cosmo/wagon/issues/30

@gsemet Your use case is the other way around from mine, as `Pipfile` is *not* my source of truth for charger dependencies, the `locked-requirements.txt` file is (and I have no interest in changing that, as I'm only using `pipenv` to manage dev environments, not the deployed charger environments - I don't even deploy wagon to the target devices, I run the unpack-and-pip-install step directly in a shell script)

Move both `Pipfile` and `Pipfile.lock` to any directory, and run `pipenv install` (or `pipenv install --dev` depending on what you want). A new virtualenv will be created. (Also you can `pipenv --rm` in the old directory before you move to delete the old virtualenv.)

oh, I just got around to reading this.  Lets look at the error message: `Could not find a version that matches eth-keys<0.2.0,<0.3.0,>=0.1.0b3,>=0.2.0b1`.  We can filter this down a bit, because `<0.2.0` is already less than `0.3.0` so we can just axe the `0.3.0` constraint, and similarly, `0.2.0b1` is greater than `0.1.0b3`.  This leaves our available package space for `eth-keys` to match against as: `>=0.2.0b1,<0.2.0`.

The versions we skip aren't super useful, because if you look carefully they are usually duplicated a bunch.  We skip lots of stuff, this is one of the reasons I wasn't totally on board with showing the listing of skipped prerelease versions in the first place.  This is super misleading -- it implies that we skipped these versions _because_ they were prerelease versions.  What actually happened is that we skipped these versions, _and also, they happened to be prerelease versions_.

I can tell you without looking at any code or doing any dependency resolution that there is no such version that will satisfy `<0.2.0, >=0.2.0b1`, so this is most likely not a bug.  It would actually be quite odd if we didn't respect the `--pre` argument because we use it ourselves all the time in our own pipfile.


Anyway I've fielded dozens of issues related to this set of ethereum tooling, the maintainers just seem to randomly pin stuff that conflicts across all of their software.  Pip doesn't have a dependency resolver, it will always install whatever you tell it to install.  That is one of the key distinguishing features of pipenv -- if you want to install 5 packages, and 3 of them try to install conflicting versions of `requests`, pipenv doesn't just let them go ahead and race to see who wins -- it will actually stop and tell you about the problem.   I'd recommend opening an issue against the packages you're trying to install and asking them to sort this out

Nope, not necessary, we don't provide a single command to both add and remove packages from the environment besides `pipenv install` with the `--python` flag (which always creates a new environment)

The idea here as I mentioned is that the environment is disposable, so we are not going through the overhead of trying to maintain its 'cleanliness' at this point.  I can see arguments in favor of doing that, but we aren't prepared to make an API change for this right now (I would reconsider it after we work out the remaining operational challenges around our semantics, which hopefully we should have in the next release or two)

Thanks @estan for your PR. It's a thorough implementation for our consideration. I'm considering it, but it does have a couple of smells that I'd like to avoid, namely that it passes a parameter through several calls, but also because that parameter has to be supplied twice (for `packages` and `namespace_packages`).

Someone recently suggested a different approach - using a sentinel file to signal a namespace package. Consider, for example, allowing a `__nspkg__` 0-length file (or similar) to declare a directory to be a namespace package. 

Such an approach would have the simple elegance of being stored with the source control system and being declarative. It would have the additional benefit of enabling the replacing the `packages` and `namespace_packages` parameters to discover these packages, such that every setup.py might look simply like:

```
setup(
  packages=find_packages(),
  namespace_packages=find_namespace_packages(),
)
```

What do the subscribers of this ticket think of such an idea?
