As a quick workaround I left out isolated seed regions, as in the code below. You can use this solution until we correct the bug.
```
from scipy import ndimage
import skimage
from skimage import filters, feature, measure
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def apply_randomwalker2D(image):
    distance = ndimage.distance_transform_edt(image)
    distance = np.array(distance)
    local_maxi = feature.peak_local_max(distance, min_distance=10, num_peaks=2,
                                indices=False, labels=image)
    markers = measure.label(local_maxi)
    markers[image==0] = -1
    fill = ndimage.binary_propagation(markers == 0, mask=markers>=0)
    isolated = np.logical_and(markers>0, np.logical_not(fill))
    markers_new = np.copy(markers)
    # Ignore isolated seeds
    markers_new[isolated] = -1
    labels_rw = skimage.segmentation.random_walker(image, markers_new,
                                                   mode='bf')
    labels_rw[labels_rw==-1]=0
    # Put back labels of isolated seeds.
    labels_rw[isolated] = markers[isolated]
    return labels_rw

def segment_z(subim):
    a = filters.gaussian(subim, 3)
    mask = a > filters.threshold_otsu(a)
    regions = apply_randomwalker2D(ndimage.binary_fill_holes(mask))
    regions[~mask]=0
    regions = skimage.segmentation.relabel_sequential(regions)[0]
    return regions

a = np.loadtxt('array.txt')
regions = segment_z(a)
plt.imshow(regions)
plt.show()
```

There is another element to getting good predictions in this case. The TM algorithm doesn't know when a sequence ends and a new one begins. This is still a matter of conjecture for neuroscience. From the algorithm standpoint, the sequence `abcd abcd abcd abcd` could be interpreted different ways:

- 4 sequences of `abcd `
- 2 sequences of `abcd abcd `
- 1 sequence of `abcd abcd abcd abcd`

As it sees more, it just gets worse for prediction simple static patterns. The current workaround is a non-biological method of programmatic resets. We allow this through `model.resetSequenceStates()`. If you call this function once a cycle, it should start recognizing individual sequences better.

Wow, you are doing a really great revamp of numexpr, which is great.  If you are serious about this, my suggestion is that you put your work on a branch (say numexpr-3.0) so that this can become a major release when ready.  Having a major release will allow to change hairy things like the treatment (or not) of Python built-in types and other possible incompatible changes.  If you are interested, tell me and I can grant you developer status in the project.

Regarding some of the questions you raised:

* Python built-in types PITA: I am +1 on dropping support for that and force the user to express everything in terms of known-size datatypes (np.int32, np.float64...).  Having a major numexpr 3.0 version will allow users to do a smooth migration in case they are using the native Python types.

* Casting problems: numexpr has some limitations in doing the casting because of the limited set of datatypes supported, and that creates some departures from numpy casting rules.  Again, I am +1 to adhere to numpy rules whenever possible and deprecate the existing ones.

As I already stated in issue #211, I won't be able to be terribly useful in implementing these new features, but I can certainly provide some guidance during the process.  Also, numexpr discussion is very welcome at the numpy mailing list, so feel free to drop some message there explaining your intentions and soliciting help from others.

Good luck

If you are building your own Python 2.7 distribution you basically need to add the _posixsubprocess.* files to the Modules/ directory, update the setup.py or Setup.pre.in files (whichever you use) to build the _posixsubprocess module, replace Lib/subprocess.py with with the subprocess32 .py file and replace the Lib/test/test_subprocess* & associated data / helper script files.

Do not do this _on top_ of an existing system such as Debian with python2.7 installed. That'll just be messed up when the package updates. You'd want to do the above within the context of building your own python2.7 interpreter package.

A solution can be found below. I monkeypatched multiprocessing run, then used the synchronous transport (the async doesn't work, because it needs to send the pending request when the process ends, but the hook isn't there).

`
import multiprocessing
import threading
import sys
from raven import Client
from raven.transport.requests import HTTPTransport

dsn = "xx"


def install_thread_excepthook():
    """
    Workaround for sys.excepthook thread bug
    (https://sourceforge.net/tracker/?func=detail&atid=105470&aid=1230540&group_id=5470).
    Call once from __main__ before creating any threads.
    If using psyco, call psycho.cannotcompile(threading.Thread.run)
    since this replaces a new-style class method.
    """
    start_old = multiprocessing.Process.run
    def run(*args, **kwargs):
        client = Client(dsn, transport=HTTPTransport)
        try:
            start_old(*args, **kwargs)
        except (KeyboardInterrupt, SystemExit):
            raise
        except:
            client.captureException()
    multiprocessing.Process.run = run

install_thread_excepthook()

def worker_function():

    print("worker crashes")
    1 / 0

def manager_function():
    workers_count = 2
    workers = [multiprocessing.Process(target=worker_function) for i in range(workers_count)]
    for worker in workers:
        worker.start()

manager_function()
`

Problem description and solution if anyone digs up this non-issue:

App was initialized like so:
```sh
uwsgi --need-app --socket 0.0.0.0:3031 --enable-threads --lazy \
    -b 30000 --single-interpreter --lazy --die-on-term \
    --module app.main --callable wsgi_main
```

With a "main" function of:
```python
import logging
 
from raven.contrib.flask import Sentry
 
from app import app
from app import config
 
sentry = None
 
def wsgi_main(environ, start_response):
    global sentry
 
    app.config.update(config.flask)
 
    if config.flask['SENTRY_DSN']:
        sentry = Sentry(
            app, dsn=config.flask['SENTRY_DSN'],
            logging=True, level=logging.ERROR
        )
 
    return app(environ, start_response)
```

The problem is that `wsgi_main` is called on every request, so Sentry wraps itself each time. The fix is to simply check if Sentry has been initialized already. Example:

```python
import logging
 
from raven.contrib.flask import Sentry
 
from app import app
from app import config
 
sentry = None
 
def wsgi_main(environ, start_response):
    global sentry
 
    app.config.update(config.flask)
 
    # change below
    if not sentry and config.flask['SENTRY_DSN']:
        sentry = Sentry(
            app, dsn=config.flask['SENTRY_DSN'],
            logging=True, level=logging.ERROR
        )
 
    return app(environ, start_response)
```

I'm finding that the handling of external `@base` values is causing some inconsistencies, which I'm pretty sure could lead to interoperability failures.

The problem is that the supplied "publicID" value is used as a base for resolving the `@context` reference, but the `@base` within the context file is used for resolving the relative `@id` URI reference in the JSON-LD source file `testcoll/d/testtype/entity1/entity-data.jsonld`.  This means that there are effectively two different base URIs being used for different purposes in the same JSON-LD file.

As before, I can't use a local (internal) `@base` with a relative reference.

If I drop the `@base` from the context file, then the supplied publicID value is used consistently throughout.

I've isolated some code and data that illustrates the issue at https://github.com/gklyne/annalist/tree/develop/spike/jsonld_context. The small python module there assumes rdflib and rdflib-jsonld are installed, and assumes the `testcoll` directory tree is present in the same directory as the Python source code.  The key data files are `testcoll/d/testtype/entity1/entity-data.jsonld` and `testcoll/d/coll_context.jsonld`.

The name of this file has its origins inside the brain of @barnabytprowe :) but you can see more about it here: https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim/pull/358 where he describes the process of producing those files.  The relevant scripts are in the GalSim repo, devel/external/hst/ if you want to check it out and make sure you do something comparable for AEGIS?

For COSMOS, he used more than one blank field, partly to beat down the noise in estimating the noise correlation function, and partly because the correlated noise varies somewhat as a function of position, so he took the average to be representative.  I would think that AEGIS is small enough that you wouldn't need as many blank fields as he used (indeed it would be hard to find so many) but you might want more than one, since the noise correlation function estimated from one field would be noisy.  Can you see how noisy your CF image is?

Hi,

Default value of max_connections in Patroni is 100.

Before bootstrapping a new cluster you have to provide the correct configuration:
```yaml
bootstrap:
  dcs:
    postgresql:
      parameters:
        max_connections: XXX
```

> I've tried to manually set a higher maximum in $datadir/postgresql.conf file, removed all configuration files, it doesn't work. 

There is a reason for that, Patroni start postgres by specifying max_connections as an command line argument. It is doing it for purpose, to avoid accidental change of parameters on one node, which have to be the same across all nodes in the cluster.

max_connections is not the only parameter, which must be the same across all nodes: https://github.com/zalando/patroni/blob/master/patroni/postgresql.py#L93-L102
and it's possible to change of those parameters only via DCS, by running `patronictl edit-config <cluster-name>` 


> I've found that max_connections setting is embedded in the control files:
> This value is generally set at 1000 instead of this weird 194 value set for debug purpose only.

It is not only stored in control file, but also replicated in wal files. Therefore it is not possible to start postgres in recovery if max_connections is smaller than it was on the master.

## Objectives

* Fusion of tightly coupled modules.
* No numpy manipulations in the openfisca_france code.
* Compatibility with the current openfisca_france code whenever possible.

## New usage

```python
import datetime

import openfisca_core
import openfisca_france.simulations
import openfisca_france.tests.base

scenario = dict(
    period=2006,
    parent1=dict(
        date_naissance=datetime.date(1972, 1, 1),
        salaire_de_base=2000,
        effectif_entreprise=25,
        categorie_salarie=openfisca_france.model.base.CAT['prive_non_cadre'],
        ),
    menage=dict(
        zone_apl=1,
        ),
    )
period = openfisca_core.periods.period(scenario['period'])

simulation = openfisca_france.simulations.Simulation(
    openfisca_france.tests.base.tax_benefit_system, scenario)

result = simulation.calculate('revdisp', period)
print((result.value, result.entity))
```

## Internal working

### Class TaxBenefitSystem

At init time, a FranceTaxBenefitSystem instance loads every openfisca_france variable with `load_variable_class()`. During this stage, some class attributes are modified and some default value are added. The column member is deleted and all its attributes are given to the variable. For all variables, a dictionary of functions indexed by periods is constructed.

At the end of the initialization, the TBS contains a dictionary of variable *classes* (not yet instantiated) indexed by names.

### Class Simulation

At initialization, a Simulation instance is given a TBS and a test case. It instantiates every variable class of its TBF to create a dictionary of variable *instances* indexed by names. It converts the test case to a vectorized format and feeds its variable instances with the resulting data.

### Class Variable

A variable instance is solely responsible for the computations and the cache management of its variable.

The result of a variable computation is a Node instance.

Additionally, a variable instance can perform relational operations on a node (split_by_roles, any_by_roles, filter_role, sum_by_entity, cast_from_entity_to_role, cast_from_entity_to_roles).

### Class Node

A node is : 
* a numpy array
* an entity. The dimention of the array is the entity count.
* a simulation object
* a default value. This is required for relational operations.

The node class overloads every numpy operation and acts as an invisible wrapper over a numpy array.

### Class Shell

If a datetime object and a node object are to be added, the __add__ operator of the datetime object will be called. Instead of raising a NotImplementedError (in which case the __radd__ operator of the Node class is called), a TypeError is raised.

The only purpose of the Shell class is to prevent delicate infix operators to be called prior to the corresponding Node operator.

### Module numpy_wrapper

Every numpy function that is used in openfisca_france and in formula helpers are wrapped in this module.

Thanks a lot @fpagnoux & @Anna-Livia for this work! 👏 

## Entities

### Beliefs

- **3a** and **3c** are out of the way for me. The person ID is ambiguous, and we definitely don't want to deal with that ambiguity. How hard will it be to debug when I'll have two `bob` persons declared in two different entities? Super hard.
- **3b** is unambiguous, but makes JSON references mandatory, which is definitely not the nicest way to walk through data paths.
- **1** and **2** mean that entities will also have to declare their pluralised key. This is acceptable.
- **2** means less depth compared to **1**.

### Opinion

I like the readability of **1**, but I prefer the writability and consolidation of **2**. Each entity type defines its specific variables, which means it is easier to have a common data model between OpenFisca and the client code.

### Conclusion

I'm in favour of **2**.

## Computation request

### Beliefs

- URL encoding does not scale. I already encountered this issue with Ludwig, it is not merely theoretical. Actually:
> URLs over 2,000 characters will not work in the most popular web browser. ([source](https://boutell.com/newfaq/misc/urllength.html))
…thus, I think **I** cannot be the _only_ way to request computations.
- It would be possible to mix **I** and **II**. There is no reason not to support both URL and payload computation requests, as long as we are consistent in the way we treat it (either merge both or send a `400` if both are requested — I would favour the latter).

### Opinion

- **III** allows for an unexpected but very interesting use case: the input and output data formats can be exactly the same, with the input merely having placeholders, thus making it very easy for the client to detect whether the computation has still to happen or not, and equally easy to invalidate a computation result.

### Open questions

- **I** and **II** do not attach requested variables to entities. How can **III** work in a similar fashion when it provides much more information?

Hey guys

Just had a quick call with @javitonino about this. So we came up with the following options in the order in which we should explore them

1. It sounds like there are some new functions in the DO API that might work as replacements for the functions that we are installing. Have asked @javitonino  to link to them in this ticket so we can take a look and see if we can use them instead. This is the preferred method as we should be leveraging the official API whenever possible.

2. If they arent going to be useful then we will explore using an anonymous function or just running the code inline somehow. The concern about adding the functions to the user accounts is that if the user gets moved to another server (which happens sometimes), the function wont come with them. I guess this is less of a problem if we check for the function existence when we instantiate the context as then we will always know its there.

3. If we cant make an anonymous function work then we will generate functions with carto_frames prefixes so they are easy to use.  

Does that sound like a plan?

I can confirm this. The change was made in this PR: https://github.com/aws/chalice/pull/506. That pull request has a lot of discussion related it to this removal. But to summarize, there was a bug inherently with having the `_body` and `_raw_body` in the result of `app.current_request.to_dict()` because the body did not have to be JSON serializeable and you would run into JSON loading errors if it was not serializeable. Furthermore, the value was prefixed with an underscore indicating that it was internal and generally should not have been used. In general, I would recommend using the `app.current_request.raw_body` to get the body of the request. Is there any particular reason that you were using `app.current_request.to_dict()` to get the body?

The thing that makes this complicated is that we need to:
1. Freeze OUR test dependency versions
1. NOT freeze any of pyca/cryptography's versions or dependency versions

A naive `pip freeze` and install from the results ends up uninstalling any mentioned dependencies that are referenced in the frozen file. This is a problem because it would result in the pyca/cryptography dev build being uninstalled and overwritten by the version that we last froze on, defeating the point of the downstream tests.

What we need to is:
1. Freeze our dependencies
1. Freeze pyca/cryptography's dependencies at that time
1. Remove all members of 2 from 1.
1. Use the resulting set for the upstream test dependencies that we store.

Because of this, we will need to maintain separate upstream test dependency files for each dependency. Currently this is only pyca/cryptography, but we should anticipate others being added in the future and make sure that whatever we do does not cause problems then.

> why you have elected not to put `__init__.py` in the root of the namespace.

For an example of the "nuts-and-bolts" of a namespace package see the [source][1] of `google/__init__.py` in the `google-cloud-datastore` package (you can download the `sdist` from [PyPI][2] and verify it is there if you like). The reason `google/__init__.py` is not present is because `pip` knows the "right way" to install a namespace package and `pip` doesn't add the file.

> BigQuery retry ignores the error code and is based solely on the reason string in the error.

Yikes. Do the error codes at least match up with idempotent http statuses?

> Should I define a special BigQueryTransientError subclass to capture that, which wraps the original error?

Yeah, that could be reasonable, something like:

```
class BigQueryTransientError(exceptions.BackendError):
     ...
```

> If so, I'll still need a custom predicate, won't I?

Possibly, if the default predicate doesn't work (e.g. if not all BackendErrors are retryable, just the subset that ends up being BigQueryTransientErrors). If that's the case, you can make it global:

```python
google.cloud.bigquery.default_retry_predicate
```

That way custom retries are a bit easier:

```python
myretry = retry.Retry(bigquery.default_retry_predicate, deadline=60)
```

You can also make the default retry object itself a global or class constant, so users can do;

```python
myretry = bigquery_client.DEFAULT_RETRY.with_deadline(60)
```

>  And won't that be worse for users when retry fails, because they'll have to call RetryError.cause() and then call cause() on that again?

If you sublcass one of the existing exception classes they should only need to call `cause()` once, unless I'm misunderstanding something.

> because of a philosophical belief about built-in language features you want to impose on other devs

It's not a philosophical belief. Pickle is objectively bad *and* insecure. While I agree that it's frustrating that these two Google products don't work together in every case, the alternative of encouraging users to put their heads in the sand while handing them the gun to shot themselves in the foot with isn't acceptable either.

Client are not serializable, nor do I think they *should* be. Several of the resources a client use (sockets, credentials, thread-locals, etc.) are inextricably tied to the current process and implementing pickle support will just lead to sneaky, unexpected bugs.

I'd recommend your DoFn construct a new client. If you're worried about the auth server being overloaded, don't, it'll give you a rate limit exceeded if you manage to surprise it. If you do, come back, I have two workarounds.

Okay, Chris, I have a branch on my fork that has the data exporter functionality. It's definitely not complete. I only have the following endpoints:

/<query_id>/download?format=csv - Which downloads a query to the format
/download - Which is used to download an SQL query from the playground. `sql` and `format` params are POSTed to this endpoint

I don't have stream or email functionality done yet. I am also not finished with the implementation. Check out `exporters.py` for my implementation so far. Subclasses of `BaseExporter` need to implement a `to_response` method that creates a response object, writes the exported data to it, and returns the response. 

I'm trying to think of ways to make this a little more generic and less coupled between exporter/response. Maybe responses could be created in a view and exporters can just be responsible for writing their data to that response and returning it? Like so:

```python
def download_view(request, query_id):
    # Get the query, add params, etc.
    exporter = ExporterClass(query)
    response = HttpResponse()
    exporter.write_to_response(response)
    return response
```

Or perhaps exporters could just write their data to a `StringIO` or `BytesIO` object and return that, then the view will write that object to the response? Like this:

```python
def download_view(request, query_id):
    # Get the query, add params, etc.
    exporter = ExporterClass(query)
    output = exporter.output()  # This returns a StringIO or BytesIO in-memory buffer
    return HttpResponse(output, content_type=exporter.content_type)
```

@ghcs27 uhh, sorry about that. When I saw this pop up in my email queue again I had a thought. While I have no problem meriging this as is, I feel like events are forcing the events and data structures code into the wild west.  So 2 thoughts that I wanted to share/discuss before merging:

As far as I remember from reviewing it, the existing data structures code was fairly spec compliant. In general, this is a good thing, because it means that you have somewhere to check if the implementation is correct. So any changes to the code, that changes it away from being spec compliant, really should be:

 1) Documenter. Can I get you to add very clear comments in the places where you are changing semantics away from the spec?
 2) Tested. Ideally, although maybe a little too late now, we should collect test cases from the issues and add those to the test suites, so that we can ensure that future changes will still allow these non-compliant events through. I don't know if you feel up to that. My fear is, that if I merge, probably it will be a little more difficult to convince bug reporters to collect test cases. On the other hand, this has also been sitting around for so long, that it might also end up annoying people that it doesn't just get merged. I'll take your input on this.

Regards Kenneth

> On another note, I think Devon wants us to avoid emitting events. @indirectlylit let me know if I misunderstood.

I didn't mean to imply that event-based communication is always bad - it's often the best way of communicating with a parent component, particularly for broadcasting about user interactions and other events.

I believe that in the case I complained about, the emitter was a modal, and it was emitting an event like `closeModal` which was a _command_ to the parent. This seemed more appropriate as an action because of the command-like nature. Typically the contract of event-driven behavior can not make assumptions about who is listening - it could be one, many, or no listeners.

@maelp There is a way to save the first STFT computation.

* Do an STFT of size 2N with zero-padding
* Compute the filter weights on the even frequencies only (So only N coefficients)
* Do interpolation of the filter weights using FFT to get 2N weights

The interpolation is done by

1. setting all the odd weights to zero
2. perform iFFT of size 2N
3. setting to zero the second half of the filter
4. doing FFT to go back to frequency domain where the odd frequencies will have been interpolated

The only extra step needed compared to a "conventional" setup is the last point which only costs 2 FFT (very small compared to cost of STFT).

This is indeed if you want to do perfect, efficient overlap-add filtering. Now, as you mention above this is rarely done with most implementation using half overlap with a Hann or Hamming window. If the filters are not too wild, the artefacts from the circular convolution will not be perceptible.

A fix for this is now on master. As opposed to your suggested change, we only try the mro lookup if `__bases__` fails to find a handler, as using `mro()` for all cases is a performance hit. The corresponding implication for performance for generic read/write handlers in transit-python is that a direct parent or e.g. a mixin pattern (anything that would succeed on a `__bases__` lookup) will be faster than inheriting past the child class level.

**cu2qu** performs a conversion of **cubic** i.e. "PostScript-type" contours into **quadratic** i.e. "TrueType-type contours" in multiple fonts at once. If you load multiple fonts into it, it iterates through all glyphs, finding the same glyph in all the fonts, and then tries to do the conversion. 

For the conversion to be successful, so that the final fonts are interpolable and can be made into a variable font, there are some key requirements: 
1. The glyph in all the fonts must have the same number of contours. 
2. Each corresponding contour in all the fonts must have the same "length" i.e. number of Bezier points (nodes and handles). 
4. The "type" of each segment (a piece of Bezier curve that goes between two nodes) in the glyph across all fonts must be of the same "type", i.e. either a curve or a line in all fonts. 

Some resources: 
* http://learn.scannerlicker.net/2014/04/16/bezier-curves-and-type-design-a-tutorial/
* https://pomax.github.io/bezierinfo/ (long but excellent if you have some background in maths)
* https://vimeo.com/116066509 — video showing http://tools.typesupply.com/prepolator.html , a tool by Tal Leming which can be used to perform "prepolation" i.e. making corresponding glyphs across multiple fonts "compatible" i.e. interpolable.

What are the trade-offs here?  My intuition is:

- lower peer count -> more performant light node assuming it finds a sufficient number of peers serving LES
- higher peer count -> client is a more *responsible* participant in the network, serving headers when it can.

I may need to think on this some more, but my intent here is more to cleanly separate this into a dedicated process so that:

1. Another unforseen blocking call like the mentioned `process()` call doesn't hang the networking connections.
2. The amount of additional `asyncio` event loop switching that has to occur in the main network process is kept to a minimum so that our networking stays quick and responsive.  

This is a step towards the place I'd like to get to where we have very strong guarantees about our networking logic not getting blocked by other logic and taking our node offline.

* A process for the database
* A process for networking
* `N` processes for the other major services we run.

@njalerikson Using symlinks on Windows is not something we can do, from the Python documentation:

`
On Windows, the SeCreateSymbolicLinkPrivilege is required in order to successfully create symlinks. This privilege is not typically granted to regular users but is available to accounts which can escalate privileges to the administrator level. Either obtaining the privilege or running your application as an administrator are ways to successfully create symlinks.
OSError is raised when the function is called by an unprivileged user.
`

It is very complicated to get that privilege on a computer on which you are an Administrator (due to Windows security token filtering).

It is (in my experience) nigh-on impossible to convince your IT department to give you that privilege on an enterprise computer.

I just notived an interesting coincidental property of the new versioning scheme: One can use `*` as a universal placeholder in version comparison. For example:

      match('1.1a', '>=1.1')   => False
      match('1.1a', '<1.1')    => True

but

      match('1.1a', '>=1.1*')  => True
      match('1.1a', '<1.1*')   => False

Is this error repeatable?  I tried to reproduce the error locally in a docker image and was not successful.  Perhaps Travis CI had a network error that made conda.anaconda.org inaccessible for a short time?

After discussion, the consensus seems to be https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/5581 is the best solution.  We just don't show a prompt modifier when the root environment is the active environment.

The root environment is still named "root." Activate works just as it did before, i.e. when no argument is given to `source activate` or `conda activate`, then it's assumed that the root environment is the one being activated.

It was discussed that the danger with not showing a prompt modifier when the root environment is active is that users can deactivate their way out of conda not being on PATH at all.  While that's also the case currently, with no indicator of the root environment being active, the prompts will look the same whether the conda root environment is active or not.

That doesn't convince me, I'm afraid. If anything it tells me anaconda-client is _too_ entangled into `conda`, or its branding needs to be removed.

I'm seeing two different things happening in your linked example. The first is the `Using Anaconda Cloud api` message. That only appears if `anaconda-client` is installed; `conda` functionality degrades gracefully if that is not the case. You lose functionality like binstar token processing but if someone relies wholly on open channels they don't need it.

The other thing is the reference to `anaconda search`. That reference should be made conditional on the existence of `anaconda-client`; at the very least, it should instruct the user to install anaconda-client to obtain that functionality. 

Alternatively, the API elements that `anaconda-client` folds into `conda` should be made more generic, so that `anaconda search` becomes `conda search`.
Either an intermittent http error, or a permissions issue.  `conda clean --packages --tarballs` might help.  If it's just an HTTP timeout, then your issue has probably already resolved itself.

> Subprocessing to `/bin/sh` or `cmd.exe` is unfortunately but cleanly the requirement of having `activate.d` scripts be `.sh` or `.bat`.

Yes, that one is unavoidable currently.

> We *do* execute each script, and if they have side effects other than environment variable changes, those side effects *will* be applied.

Yes, I can't think of any relevant changes that won't be applied. Some changes like function definitions or `set` but not `export`ed variables (activation scripts are `source`d!) won't survive with the current approach -- but these are irrelevant either way since we want to `exec` the target. (IMO, `conda run` really doesn't have to guarantee to be able to execute an equivalent of obscure cases like `(conda activate env && some_shell_func_from_activate_script)` and a `conda run -p /env0 conda activate /env1` doesn't make much sense either.)

> The alternative you suggest is to fork a python process, exec to a generated script to activate, and then exec again to the target executable.

I think we have these options:
```
#1
conda run (python)
|\
| (shell)
| |\
| | conda shell.posix activate (python)
| |/
| |- eval activate scripts (shell, same process)
| |\
| | python -c 'import os, json; print(json.dumps(dict(os.environ)))'
| |/
|/
exec target

#2
python (conda run)
|\
| (shell)
| |\
| | conda shell.posix activate (python)
| |/
| |- eval activate scripts (shell, same process)
| |
| exec python -c 'import os, json; print(json.dumps(dict(os.environ)))'
| |
|/
exec target

#3
conda run (python)
|
|\
| conda shell.posix activate (python)
|/
|- add 'exec target' to shell script (python, same process)
|
exec composed script (shell)
|
|- eval activate scripts (shell, same process)
|
exec target

#4
conda run (python)
|
|- conda shell.posix activate (python, same process)
|
|- add 'exec target' to shell script (python, same process)
|
exec composed script (shell)
|
|- eval activate scripts (shell, same process)
|
exec target

#5
conda run (python)
|\
| (shell)
| |\
| | conda shell.posix activate (python)
| |/
| |- eval activate scripts (shell, same process)
| |
| exec target
|/
exit <-- ugly, waiting process...

#6
conda run (shell) <-- less flexible, e.g. needs to parse argv etc., in shell...
|\
| conda shell.posix activate (python)
|/
|- eval activate scripts (shell, same process)
|
exec target
```
- `#1` is the current state of the PR, if I'm not missing anything.
- `#2` is a very minor, trivial change to avoid another fork (but that one is not important at all).
- `#3` is nicer since we can directly invoke the `python`->`exec shell`->`exec target` chain.
- `#4` improves `#3` so that no fork (and costly `python` call with `conda.context` init) is necessary.
- `#5` has a waiting process :-1:.
- `#6` is the less flexible `conda-run.sh` approach.

IMO, `#1` or `#2` are fine as an initial implementation, `#3` needs some additional changes but would be nice to have and `#4` is my favorite but needs some more (minor?) changes to make `conda.activate` work nicely from within an existing `conda` process.

> I would be shocked if that optimization gains more than 50 ms.

I expect something in the ballpark of 100-150 ms, maybe, which is may be a factor 2-3 more, but is still not too far off considering the overall process would be < 300 ms, I think. And if a user wants to run it in a tight loop, then they're likely doing something wrong anyway..

@kalefranz @willingc got to the bottom of this: the issue is caused by [RHEL 7 / CentOS 7 not supporting the Docker `overlay` storage driver](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-US/Red_Hat_Enterprise_Linux/7/html/7.2_Release_Notes/technology-preview-file_systems.html), which is the new Docker default. > However, we do not want to do that with BLAS, nor the other packages we are considering variant metapackages for, because we want to allow the packages to be installed side-by-side, even though they are not used at the same time.

Not sure I agree with this point on BLAS. I watched a number of things break recently when OpenBLAS linked things and MKL linked things interacted with each other. Most notably I saw this with NumPy and SciPy. Don't think I want to encourage mixing of BLAS. As for other stuff, I cannot say whether this is ok or not.

Ok that makes sense then.  If you've just got conda on PATH, you're not using the Anaconda Prompt, and not running `activate root` as a first step after opening `cmd.exe`, the conda activate script wouldn't have the opportunity to set `PYTHONIOENCODING` for you.

I am thinking that each carray should have it's own lock object (instance
of threading.Lock) which should be acquired within any method modifying
underlying data, which I think comprises __setitem__, append, trim and
resize. This means that the GIL could safely be released for calls to
blosc_compress_ctx, allowing other threads to carry on, but preventing
simultaneous attempts to modify data. I'm not hugely experienced with this
sort of thing so advice very welcome.

After further reflection I do not think using `--list` and `--files` for communicating with Sigasi is a good idea for the future and would rather see a `--export-to-json` flag to export data from VUnit to other tools. This means I do not think we should merge #365.

My reasons are as follows:
1. The `--list` and `--files` are mutually exclusive which means you need two invocations of `run.py` to get both. For large projects the `run.py` can be a bit sluggish especially if users add a lot of data-generation.

2. The `--list` and `--files` flags are Machine-to-User and not Machine-to-Machine and can change based on estetics etc. Maybe we would like to add color or extra information in the future. This would break the Sigasi integration.

Based on this reasons I think we should define a Machine-to-Machine data format for `--export-to-json` that conveys all information that Sigasi needs as well as a version of the format if we need to change it in the future.

Sorry to make this more complicated for you but I think we will be thankful in the future.

> How do I go about specifying a custom ARM endpoint to target?

To specify a custom ARM endpoint, you can register a new cloud:
```
$ az cloud register -n CloudWithCustomEndpoint --endpoint-active-directory https://login.microsoftonline.com --endpoint-active-directory-graph-resource-id https://graph.windows.net/ --endpoint-active-directory-resource-id https://management.core.windows.net/ --endpoint-gallery https://gallery.azure.com/ --endpoint-management https://management.core.windows.net/ --endpoint-resource-manager <YOUR_CUSTOM_ENDPOINT> --suffix-storage-endpoint core.windows.net
```

Afterwards, switch to that:
```
$ az context create --cloud CloudWithCustomEndpoint -n CustomCloud
```

List the registered clouds with `az cloud list`:

$ az cloud list -otable
Name

AzureGermanCloud
CloudWithCustomEndpoint

Switch to AzureChinaCloud for example:
```
$ az context create --cloud AzureChinaCloud -n MyChina
```
The endpoints will have changed so then you can log in if needed.
```
$ az login
To sign in, use a web browser to open the page https://aka.ms/deviceloginchina ...
```

    --dtu 200 --edition Standard
```
but the following succeeded:
```
az sql elastic-pool update --id "/subscriptions/xxxxx/resourceGroups/Our-RG/providers/Microsoft.Sql/servers/servername/elasticPools/server-pool" \
    --dtu 200 --edition Standard --max-size 50GB --db-dtu-max 200
```
In this case, it was just a matter of adding the required options.

Two people hit this so far.  Two possible solutions:
1. by default for command "az ad sp create-for-rbac", assign "--role contributor --scopes /subscriptions/SUBSCRIPTIONID"
2. give an error if the customer does not set role or scopes (and override with --force if they really want no roles or scopes)

workaround is using `az resource update` command
```
az resource update -g DEMO-FTAPP --namespace "Microsoft.Network/trafficManagerProfiles" \
	--resource-type "azureEndpoints" --api-version 2015-04-28-preview \
	--parent "ilkimftapptm" -n endpoint1 \
	--set properties.priority=1 --verbose
```
notice that `azureEndpoints` is used for `-resource-type` value, not `Microsoft.Network/trafficManagerProfiles/azureEndpoints`

We do not have a good generic way of ensuring that resources are not overwritten. Only some resources support this functionality. We are trying very hard to avoid different semantics for the same verb in the CLI. 

The scenario for "recreating the zone" is to write resilient scripts. If your script creates a zone in step 1 and then something else in step 2, but step 2 fails, you want to be able to re-run your script. The proposed change makes that harder. 

In my opinion, we should open a generic issue for create commands in general to track the overwrite on create semantics and either make sure it works across the board, or we keep the behavior as-is.

A quick update, I found this python library [trimesh](https://github.com/mikedh/trimesh) to be extremely useful. If the [v-hacd](https://github.com/kmammou/v-hacd) library is already installed, then update the environment variable PATH to the testVHACD executable. Once that is done, 

```
import trimesh
from trimesh.io.urdf import export_urdf
mesh = trimesh.load(<path to STL/ OBJ/PLY file>)
export_urdf(mesh, <folder path to which urdf has to be saved>)
```
This will do a hierarchial decomposition of the concave object and generate a urdf file from the same. This urdf file can be easily used in bullet. 

I am on Ubuntu and hence I compiled the v-hacd. My export path is as follows:

` export PATH=$PATH:' <path to v-hacd>/build/linux2/test`

Note: The v-hacd have the binary for OSx and Windows, for Linux it has to be compiled using the the run.py in the scripts folder and then running make in the build directory. The build directory will be automatically created upon successful running of run.py

Hi, the variability in the outputs has to do with parameter initialization. In the current implementation emission probabilities are initialized randomly, which makes the learning converge to slightly different results.

Another issue is that initial parameters are unused, because a) they are missing _ suffix and b) `init_param` is missing from the constructor call. Here's the corrected version:

```python
import numpy as np
from hmmlearn import hmm

states = ["Rainy", "Sunny"]
n_states = len(states)

observations = ["walk", "shop", "clean"]
n_observations = len(observations)

model = hmm.MultinomialHMM(n_components=n_states, init_params="")
model.startprob_ = np.array([0.6, 0.4])
model.transprob_ = np.array([
  [0.7, 0.3],
  [0.4, 0.6]
])
model.emissionprob_ = np.array([
  [0.1, 0.4, 0.5],
  [0.6, 0.3, 0.1]
])

# predict a sequence of hidden states based on visible states
bob_says = np.array([[0, 2, 1, 1, 2, 0]]).T

model = model.fit(bob_says)
logprob, alice_hears = model.decode(bob_says, algorithm="viterbi")
print("Bob says:", ", ".join(map(lambda x: observations[x], bob_says)))
print("Alice hears:", ", ".join(map(lambda x: states[x], alice_hears)))
```

I'm not sure to understand the necessity of `Content-Disposition`, though. Could you help me out, here?

From what I read on the [Amazon S3 docs](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/API/RESTObjectPUT.html#RESTObjectPUT-requests-request-headers) and the [W3C specs](https://www.w3.org/Protocols/rfc2616/rfc2616-sec19.html#sec19.5.1), this is meant for clients to present the data differently (like changing a file name). Because this is not part of the HTTP specs, this is a nice to have, in my opinion.

Hence, forcing this header as a default on POST/PUT requests doesn't seem natural to me. Moreover, this might also represent a security risk. For example, one may not want to share the actual file name stored on a local disk. I also don't see a way filter out this header (with `skip_auto_header` for instance) before the request is sent.

[`@fperez` commented](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/issues/805#issuecomment-3772232): There's something I want to add here that I've been cooking in my head but so far have only mentioned to @stefanv at a whiteboard...  a different kind of 'cell groups'.

The one thing where even the notebook doesn't work very well if you want to mix text-based explanations with code, is the definition of complex objects.  The reason is a limitation imposed by Python itself: Python does not allow you to declare a class in multiple execution chunks (well, you can, but only using dynamic hacks that aren't very readable and which I will henceforth ignore).

This means that if we start using the notebook for declaring more complex classes, right now they have to live in a single, long cell.  This prevents you for example from inserting a text paragraph with a mathematical explanation before continuing with the next method.

It occurred to me that if we had a concept of a 'cell group' where all the cells in the group would be _unconditionally_ executed _always together, all of them_, then we'd solve this problem.  Consider creating a cell group as stated above, then when you hit Shift-Enter from any cell in the group, we actually send for execution the union of all the code cells in the group ignoring non-code ones.  Then, Python would always see them as a single cell, while the human could break up the visual layout between code and text as best fit the problem.

There are some UI challenges in how to best do this, but the more I think about it the more I like this idea.  It would make the notebook a very nice environment for literate programming in a way that no existing python tool allows.  Thoughts?

So, `MultiLingualString`. It made sense at the time. 😀 For context, I wrote that when I was at a company who made a popular realtime game. Our use case was that we'd fetch a deeply nested MongoDB object with many localized strings holding things like table titles, text fields, multiple choice answers, etc. We'd cache that once and then for each client who asked for it we'd serialize the object with their locale.

The thinking at the time was that this required far less code and was less error-prone than writing an external export loop that reimplemented Schematics own working one.

On the one hand, I'm proud of how nicely MLS solved our problem. On the other, I haven't personally used it in a couple of years and I'm not highly motivated to rewrite it today to solve a problem I'm not sure anyone else has.

All the PRs for basic E2E support are now opened! Here are some instructions in order to try it out (and help track down eventual bugs).

### Disclaimer
The implementation is mostly unreviewed, and shouldn't be trusted.  
There are still missing features compared to the JS SDK, such as encrypted attachments, device verification and key sharing.

### Installation
The Olm C library is needed. It can be installed from [source](https://git.matrix.org/git/olm/), or with a package manager ([Archlinux](https://aur.archlinux.org/packages/libolm/), [Debian](https://packages.debian.org/sid/libolm2)...).
Then, use the branch https://github.com/Zil0/matrix-python-sdk/tree/e2e_beta_1, where I'll only push fixes in the future. You will also have to get the optional E2E dependencies. For example, using pip (preferably inside a virtualenv): `pip install -e 'git+https://github.com/Zil0/matrix-python-sdk@e2e_beta_1#egg=matrix-python-sdk-e2e[e2e]' --process-dependency-links`

### Using encryption
In order to enable encryption support, the only modification needed is to pass `encryption=True` when instantiating `MatrixClient`.  
However, you should also keep track of the device id of the client, otherwise a new one will be generated by Synapse on every launch.

### Sample code
Assuming we can join the existing room `#test:localhost`:
```
from matrix_client.client import MatrixClient

user_id = "@test:localhost"
password = "test"
device_id = "device_id"
room_id = "#test:localhost"

client = MatrixClient("http://localhost:8008", encryption=True)
client.login(username=user_id, password=password, device_id=device_id)
room = client.join_room(room_id)

if not room.encrypted:
    room.send_text("Unencrypted!")
    room.enable_encryption()
    room.send_text("Encrypted, if we had the required power level.")
client.logout()

client = MatrixClient("http://localhost:8008", encryption=True)
client.login(username=user_id, password=password, device_id=device_id)
room = client.join_room(room_id)
room.send_text("Reusing the same device ID should allow good performance and keep Riot "
               "from complaining.")
client.logout()
```

### Finding and reporting bugs
Most bugs will include undecryptable sent or received messages. There are a lot of different possible causes, which might be hard to track down if they are race conditions.  
When a bug is mostly reproducible, I welcome a report along with a log at least of INFO level, and explanations on how to trigger it. An issue with those info can be opened against my [fork](https://github.com/Zil0/matrix-python-sdk/).

The end of my GSoC has come. I'll stay around, get everything merged and fix bugs, but this is a good opportunity to post an update!

The new branch https://github.com/Zil0/matrix-python-sdk/tree/e2e_beta_2 is available for anyone wanting to test E2E.

The instructions are alike to those above, except there is a new feature that spares having to keep track of device ID. Simply do `client = MatrixClient(host, encryption=True, restore_device_id=True)` to enable it.

All the missing features are also out since the last update (encrypted attachments, device verification, key sharing, import/export of room keys). There might be some rough edges, let me know!

Last but not least, I've written up a file which explains more of what can be done with E2E. It should be present at the root of the repo as `E2E_overview.rst`.

Now that s3am supports downloads, all URLs referring to objects in S3 should use the `s3://` scheme and be implemented by calling s3am. That should be fairly easy to do as all machinery is already there. You can rip out the encryption header stuff. As a beneficial side-effect, the downloads are going to be much faster and more reliable. The general rules for running s3am programmatically as documented on its README apply.

If the user insists on passing a region-specific, public or signed HTTP(S) url pointing at an object in S3 that should also support, minus encryption. It would be the responsibility of the user to fabricate HTTP(S) urls that can be curled without adding query parameters to the URL or setting headers when requesting them.

The ICGC storage system will NOT be addressed using s3:// URL's, but some other scheme like icgc://.

The methods inside a class are always internal.
I think you should use the Analysis object, from there on it is clear if the call is internal or external.

I just wrote a short demo how this could work:
```
from androguard.misc import AnalyzeAPK
from androguard.core.analysis.analysis import ExternalMethod
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import networkx as nx

a, d, dx = AnalyzeAPK("4e2201cde26141715255d2421f0bcfb1")

CFG = nx.DiGraph()

# Note: If you create the CFG from many classes at the same time, the drawing
# will be a total mess...
for m in dx.find_methods(classname="Lcom/elite/MainActivity;"):
    orig_method = m.get_method()
    print("Found Method --> {}".format(orig_method))
    # orig_method might be a ExternalMethod too...
    # so you can check it here also:
    if isinstance(orig_method, ExternalMethod):
        is_this_external = True
        # If this class is external, there will be very likely
        # no xref_to stored! If there is, it is probably a bug in androguard...
    else:
        is_this_external = False

    CFG.add_node(orig_method, external=is_this_external)

    for other_class, callee, offset in m.get_xref_to():
        if isinstance(callee, ExternalMethod):
            is_external = True
        else:
            is_external = False

        if callee not in CFG.node:
            CFG.add_node(callee, external=is_external)

        # As this is a DiGraph and we are not interested in duplicate edges,
        # check if the edge is already in the edge set.
        # If you need all calls, you probably want to check out MultiDiGraph
        if not CFG.has_edge(orig_method, callee):
            CFG.add_edge(orig_method, callee)

nx.draw_networkx(CFG)
plt.draw()
plt.show()
```

More general also about my compiler fixes: An invalid query resulting with SalesforceError is currently still much more probable than an incorrect valid query with bad results, if the query is outside the implemented scope. This was good. I think that the NotImplementedError should be raised for too complicated cases that could be simpiified by any patch to anything valid but incorrect. It is frequently easier to write a correct complicated query manually than to manually verify the correctness of an automatically created query.

I think that you can compile an equivalent query with the latest master by

    OpportunityLineItem.objects.filter(has_schedule=..., opportunity__stage_name=...,
            opportunity__probability__gt=...,
            (Q(opportunity__close_categte=...) | Q(opportunity__mame__contains=...)),
            opportunity__amount__gt=...)

I want also to commit a few older changesets soon that allows to compile queries with a complicated relationship tree, e.g. a filter for a person by the FirstName of paternal grandmother and Birthdate of maternal grandfather: `filter(father__mother__first_name=..., mother__father__birthdate__gt=...)`

So in order to invoke ``2_to_3`` automated convertor on ``avro-python2`` during install we have to modify it's ``setup.py`` file. There is no way to it from cwltool's ``setup.py`` file.

But the changes we need to make to avro-python2 ``setup.py`` are very minimal for it convert all the source files to Python 3. Here's the link to the ``setup.py`` and below is the diff: 

``` diff
 try:
-  from setuptools import setup
+  from setuptools import setup, find_packages
 except ImportError:
   from distutils.core import setup
 from sys import version_info
@@ -27,10 +27,10 @@ if version_info[:2] <= (2, 5):
 
 setup(
   name = 'avro',
-  version = '@AVRO_VERSION@',
-  packages = ['avro',],
-  package_dir = {'avro': 'src/avro'},
-  scripts = ["./scripts/avro"],
+  version = '1.0',
+  package_dir = find_packages(),
+  use_2to3=True,
 
   #include_package_data=True,
   package_data={'avro': ['LICENSE', 'NOTICE']},
```

``2to3`` might introduce regressions. But I've tested it with ``schema_salad`` and ``cwltool`` and it passes all ``unit`` tests in Python 3.

I read an interesting article the other day about writing good code, can't find the link now though. One of the things it wrote about was not having all these if-switches in a function or class. If you find that your function/class is doing multiple things and needs if-statements to separate between these things, it's probably better to split it in multiple functions/classes.

I think that also applies here. We have a single `Tooltip` class that does two different things: render normal text and render geojson/topojson data fields. There are switches in multiple places to distinguish between the two behaviors. Users have to be careful to use the right arguments for their usecase.

So I want to make the case for splitting the class in two: one for simple text tooltips and one for more complicated geojson/topojsons. I'll open a PR on your branch again @jtbaker so you can check it out. I haven't tested it yet so please review.

* Taking in part_size via command line argument with upload file, added to doc string

* Checks if stream exceeds max number of parts, 10000, and raises exception.
* Checks if stream exceeds max file size of 10TB, and raises exception.

More details about the use case - I was trying to refactor the internals of a system without breaking API contracts. I use marshmallow as a way to keep the contract stable. All data flows through schemas before going out. In the API and corresponding data models, there were 3-4 different schemas with fields like country_id, department_id, municipality_id but since all the other fields were matching, I could consolidate a ton of models into one single model with a location_id. Then at the marshmallow layer I retrofit everything to the old API contract by converting location_id back into country_id, department_id or whatever is required.

Hi Victor,

Please, take a look on this page - http://python-dependency-injector.ets-labs.org/containers/index.html. Basically, container - is a group of providers.

> Why not just a class with fields initialized to providers 

`DeclarativeContainer` is just a class which fields are initialized to providers. One of the `DeclarativeContainer` benefits is that it provides also a dictionary of all declared providers:

```python
"""Declarative IoC containers inheritance example."""

import dependency_injector.containers as containers
import dependency_injector.providers as providers


class ContainerA(containers.DeclarativeContainer):
    """Example IoC container A."""

    provider1 = providers.Factory(object)


class ContainerB(ContainerA):
    """Example IoC container B."""

    provider2 = providers.Singleton(object)


# Making some asserts for `providers` attribute:
assert ContainerA.providers == dict(provider1=ContainerA.provider1)
assert ContainerB.providers == dict(provider1=ContainerA.provider1,
                                    provider2=ContainerB.provider2)

# Making some asserts for `cls_providers` attribute:
assert ContainerA.cls_providers == dict(provider1=ContainerA.provider1)
assert ContainerB.cls_providers == dict(provider2=ContainerB.provider2)

# Making some asserts for `inherited_providers` attribute:
assert ContainerA.inherited_providers == dict()
assert ContainerB.inherited_providers == dict(provider1=ContainerB.provider1)
```

This feature is useful when you want to attach some common functionality for a group of providers. Let's consider next example:

Z3 uniformly uses a less efficient SAT solver for the multi-objective case. It also creates a Pseudo-Boolean constraint after solving the first objective. So this could explain the big difference.  This constraint also influences solving time for the second problem. I used to implement a much weaker version of lexicographic composition which simply hardened the soft constraints from the first objective. This approach would be faster, but not produce optimal values for the second objective. 
So the semantics is different the output depends heavily on which solution is found in the first round.

There are some better approaches, http://link.springer.com/article/10.1007%2Fs10472-011-9233-2, but I have not yet examined support for these, but good use-cases could help drive adapting better solutions.

The way the datatype decision procedure performs splitting on recursive data-types is a decision procedure when the formulas are quantifier-free. For formulas with quantifiers, no termination guarantees are provided. The example exhibits a case where the procedure that builds a finite model
for quantifier-free recursive data-types fails to terminate. The quantified formula keeps getting instantiated by freshly generated terms, and the instantiation causes the decision procedure to create fresh terms that cause additional instantiation.

What do you guys think about something like this? These functions are supposed to replace the existing `Objective` classes:

```python
def objective(input_layer, loss_function=mse, aggregation='mean',
              input=None, target=None, **kwargs):
    y = get_output(input_layer, input, **kwargs)
    # create a target variable the same shape as the output if none is
    # specified.
    t = y.type('target') if target is None else target

    losses = loss_function(y, t)

    if aggregation == 'mean':
        loss = losses.mean()
    elif aggregation == 'sum':
        loss = losses.sum()
    else:
        raise ValueError('aggregation must be \'mean\' or \'sum\', '
                         'got {0}'.format(aggregation))

    if target is None:
        return loss, t
    else:
        return loss


def masked_objective(input_layer, loss_function=mse, aggregation='mean',
                     input=None, target=None, mask=None, **kwargs):
    y = get_output(input_layer, input, **kwargs)
    # create a target variable the same shape as the output if none is
    # specified.
    t = y.type('target') if target is None else target
    losses = loss_function(y, t)
    m = losses.type('mask') if mask is None else mask
    masked_losses = losses * m

    if aggregation == 'mean':
        loss = masked_losses.mean()
    elif aggregation == 'sum':
        loss = masked_losses.sum()
    elif aggregation == 'normalized_sum':
        loss = masked_losses.sum() / mask.sum()
    else:
        raise ValueError('aggregation must be \'mean\', \'sum\' or '
                         '\'normalized_sum\','
                         ' got {0}'.format(aggregation)) 

    if target is None and mask is None:
        return loss
    else:
        ret = [loss]
        if target is not None:
            ret.append(t)
        if mask is not None:
            ret.append(m)
        return ret
```

As I've mentioned before, the main advantage to the `Objective` class for me is symmetry: I don't have to worry about the input variable because the `InputLayer` creates it for me, and I don't have to worry about the target variable because the `Objective` creates it for me.

So this alternative `objective` utility function is also able to create a target variable if you don't specify one, and will return it along with the loss expression if this is the case. Otherwise it will just return the loss expression (since you don't need an extra reference to the target variable you passed).

The classes don't have any real state, so I think these functions can do everything the classes can do.

Feedback is welcome. Once we settle in an interface I'll make a PR.

> Re: `split()` as a helper function in proposal C2: could it not be a method on a layer with multiple outputs instead? That would be more natural imo.

It would if we could add a base class for multiple-output layers, but we can't.

> Especially because we would probably want `lasagne.layers.split()` as an alias for `SplitLayer`

I envision that nobody would use `SplitLayer` anyway, but either use `split(layer)[key]` or `layer[key]` if we implement `__index()__`. We could also overload `split(layer, key=None)` so it can be used both as a `SplitLayer` shortcut with a single defined key, or for exploding the layer.

> What if you want to mix and match, i.e. have two of the inputs be provided by a single layer, and then a third by another layer?

You could just use something like `MergeLayer(split(layer1) + [layer2])`. Unfortunately, Python dictionaries do not support the `+` operator, so you'd need some acrobatics in that case.

> Thanks a lot for the clear overview of different options. I haven't really decided yet which one I'd be in favour of.

Me neither. It's a tradeoff between complicating the `get_output()` logic and complicating usage.
Option (C) would be straightforward in terms of the implementation, but all those (implicitly created) `SplitLayer` instances might be confusing to users.
Option (B) would avoid the asymmetry of having a special layer class for multiple inputs, but no special class for multiple outputs. It would invite user errors, though, because it would be less clear which layers deal with dictionaries, lists or single tensors. Maybe they would have to signal that to the base class constructor. And for consistency, we would have to turn existing `MergeLayer` subclasses into `Layer` subclasses, endangering backwards compatibility (I'd need to think this through).
Option (A) complicates `get_output()` to avoid some `SplitLayer` instances. It still wouldn't make explicit which layers produce multiple outputs, but at least we could throw a meaningful error if an ordinary layer (rather than a merge layer) is stacked on a multiple-output layer, by just checking the incoming shape in the `Layer` base class constructor. Something like "XYLayer only accepts a single input, but you stacked it on top of a ASDFLayer producing 4 outputs: 'foo', 'bar', 'baz', 'bal'. Either index the ASDFLayer with square brackets to select one of the inputs, or replace XYLayer by a subclass of MergeLayer. Also check the documentation of ASDFLayer, maybe it can be configured to produce a single output only."

I'm still slightly favoring option (B), or would at least like to ponder it some more to see whether it would be possible. It seems elegant to me to use `MergeLayer` merely for merging multiple layers and nothing else.

> I like the idea of making multiple output layers indexable very much though.

I think this is something we can do without endangering the API, and independently of what we decide above. The only other thing that indexing could mean would be slicing the output, and this is something we could still add on top (by checking whether `self.output_shape` is a single shape or multiple shapes).

Jan writes:
> Let's say I've got Lasagne installed with python setup.py develop, 
> and that other code that depends on Lasagne doesn't even have a 
> setup.py, but is just part of an experiment specification (similar to 
> a Pylearn2 config file). Should I have a requirements.txt for every 
> config file to record the commit of Lasagne I had when writing it?
> 
I'd say clearly yes if you want to make sure your experiment is truly 
reproducible, even in three months' time.  With only a minimum 
requirement, or similarly, with "latest Git", it's too easy to break 
things otherwise.  Even in very subtle ways, e.g. if Lasagne changed 
the default nonlinearity.

This is what Sander's team writes in their ndsb docs:

> We recommend installing the following commit, which our code is known 
> to work with: ​f445b71

If they said "at least f445b71", their instructions would likely stop 
working soon.
> Should my training script collect the different requirements.txt 
> files and do something with it before running?
> 
I'd tell your users to run pip install -r requirements.txt before 
running the training script (or else, rely on them to know what they're 
doing).

pip freeze should help with writing the requirements file.  It allows 
you to collect all requirements at the time you write the experiment in 
one file.

> Soo... I don't feel that strongly about pep440-style versions, I just 
> thought they'd be very nice to have.
> 
The wording here seems wrong.  This issue is not a matter of being 
pep440 compatible or not, i.e. we're perfectly pep440 compatible 
without the git sub-versions feature suggested here.
> If the majority is against it, we can just have a fixed __version__ = 
> 'something' line in __init__.py that's set to a version number for 
> every release, and to 'bleeding-edge' for every commit that's not a 
> release.
> 
I think the better thing to do here is to use the following in 
__init__.py so that it's in sync with what version is defined in 
setup.py:

> import pkg_resources
> __version__ = pkg_resources.get_distribution("Lasagne").version

And then in setup.py follow pep440, where proper release version 
numbers are e.g. '1.0' and development versions (i.e. our versions in 
Git) have e.g. '1.0.dev' (which would be the development version that 
comes before 1.0, according to the PEP).   The end result wouldn't be 
much different to what you're suggesting, it's just more in line with 
what tools like pip would expect.  (A version that's called 
'bleeding-edge' is broken as far as pip and pep440 are concerned.)

Yeah I think the status bar will probably be way different for a web app. I'm guessing the `<Notebook />` component shouldn't even have the status bar - it should be some higher component up that looks more like this for the opinionated electron notebook (note: as structure, not as the actual components):

<App>
  <Notebook />
  <StatusBar />
</App>

This is almost there. The release goes as follows:

- Create a branch for the release called 'release-6.8.5' where the numbers correspond to the desired release version.
- This branch is brought up to date with develop + the release notes in the template.
- When all is ready we comment `release` to trigger the build.
- The build runs the release script `startRelease.sh` inside a virtualenv. This script takes care of release numbers (from the branch name), moving release notes, creating a tag for the release and uploading to pypi. At the end the development flag is set back to true and the branch is pushed back to github with all the changes.
- We merge the PR for the release branch into develop.
- Back to working on new features.

@computron, I did some reasearch into the structure features in above paper. I'm going to implement GRDF, incorporating PRDF into the featurizer, along with AFS. Summary below is mostly for my benifit.


PRDF (partial radial distribution function)  vector
- already implemented
- need to update return section of doc

GRDF (general radial distribution function)  vector
- similar to AGNIFingerprints
- PRDF and AGNIFingerprints (baring direction resolved features) could be implemented inside of GRDF
- GRDF uses a generalized distance functional for binning (atom can belong to more than one bin, and its weight need not be one)
- suggested bins: gaussians, trig functions, bessel functions
- should implement site-wise not element-pair-wise

AFS (angular fourier series)  2x2 matrix
- also uses generalized distance metrics (could share with GRDF)
- each element is two generalized distance metrics multiplied by angle

BOP (bond orientation parameter)  vector
- first few terms implemented as q2, q4, and q6 in OPSiteFingerprint
- metric for nearest neighbor enviornments

TensorBoard just does "import tensorflow" from its python code, nothing fancier than that.  So it will pick up whatever TensorFlow version you have available in your python environment.

If you want to use the custom-compiled tensorflow you'd probably need to custom-compile a whole pip package and install that locally; I don't think there's a way for TensorBoard to refer to libtensorflow.so directly.  That said, in typical usage TensorBoard doesn't use any parts of TensorFlow that would particularly benefit from the special processor instructions, so it should be fine to just ignore the warning - it's really just pointing out a potential optimization but doesn't affect the functioning of TensorFlow.

So If you're having other issues getting TensorBoard working, those almost certainly aren't related to the warning message.

This PR support simultaneous joining via single joiner router. Just for short period of time the joiner router intentionally drops some frames from a commissioner. Because of lost response, the joiner would retransmit it's request (a few times) and the commissioner would retransmit response that should be processed by the joiner router this time.

If we want to keep a few simultaneous JOIN_ENT transactions, we should provide a few KEK slots in the KeyManager - we don't know in which order JOIN_ENT.rsp messages will arrive. JOIN_ENT transaction is very short (single request - response, one hop). That's why I suggest handling it one by one instead of overcomplicating the code to make this very rare case a little faster.

The above is a work around, but not a real fix (it still violates section 10.6.2 of the Thread Spec)
After a message M triggered address resolution because the destination address was an ML-EID, M has to be changed in two spots before sending it:
1. Change the MAC80154 address to short address obtained from the address resolution (as it is currently done)
2. Replace the IPv6 address (ML-EID) by the RLOC.
With the above changes the fix in #1653, is valid.

One should also consider not using the ML-EID as source address, because this triggers Address resolution in the receiver before it can reply to the originator's request

Okay, so I think there are two issues here, and I am working on a fix.

Issue 1: we don't recognize an existing instance when returning a base class pointer != instance pointer (i.e. due to the base class being the non-first parent somewhere in the inheritance tree).

Issue 2: when we return a base class pointer, we correctly detect (via run-time `typeid`) that it is a derived type, but then we `reinterpret_cast` the pointer to the derived type--but that isn't valid when the base class pointer is offset, as in issue 1.

The crux of this entire issue is that "tss key". In Python 2.7, it's called `autoTLSkey` (https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/baca85fcc7cdf70af4a76ea0966d4842c173de1a/Python/pystate.c#L599), and it's declared static inside `pystate.c` (https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/baca85fcc7cdf70af4a76ea0966d4842c173de1a/Python/pystate.c#L40), so external software can't get at it and know its value. (In practice, it is almost certainly going to be 0, but we can't rely on this behavior.) The `internals.tstate` is a separate key. The two APIs don't know about the other one's key, and so they end up creating two separate thread states for the same thread. Python has internal `assert()`s which check for that specific condition (they only trigger if you have  debug build of Python.)

The first example I gave was when I was in the earlier stages of tracking down this issue. It gets triggered because Python internally uses the `PyGILState_*` API when it creates a pythread. The second example with `PyGILState_*` more accurately illustrates our use case. The software that uses `PyGILState_*` is 3rd party, so we don't have a good way of changing it. Our software is large and complex, and has long-running operations in C++ land, so it always releases the GIL when it leaves Python land and reacquires it when it reenters, hence the release followed by acquire that I had in the main thread in my original example. That release and acquire, coupled with the debug build in Python, was crucial to trigger the `assert()` error.

But back to my original point: none of this would be an issue if we had access to `autoTLSkey`. Before #1286, I did experiment with trying to fix the advanced API. I made it first check `PyGILState_GetThisThreadState()`. The problem is that this breaks the thread disassociation feature of the advanced API: since we don't have access to `autoTLSkey`, we can't cut the relationship between the thread and the thread state the way the advanced API does. At that point, I decided not to try to fix the advanced API and to instead just give the user the option to switch between the advanced API and `PyGILState_*`, because our team doesn't need any of the features of the advanced API, and we would prefer to just use `PyGILState_*`. So that's where we're at now.

If we can find a way to get the advanced API to use whatever thread state was created with `autoTLSkey` and also be able to cut that relationship to preserve the thread disassociation feature, we can put this whole thing to bed. I currently don't have any ideas on how to do that other than hard-coding a value of 0 for the predicted value of `autoTLSkey`.

Specifically, this is what you are looking for:

py::class_<Article> article(m, "Article").def_readwrite("images", (py::opaque<Article::image_list_type> Article:: *) & Article:: images);
```
(For pretty much all common things, it shouldn't be necessary to touch the C API directly).

The commands used to compile and link look wrong to me:

> `cd /root/builds/builts/hypertable/src/cc/HyperPython && /usr/bin/c++ ... -I/usr/local/include/python2.7 ...`

is caused because you're already called `find_package(Python)` in the root CMakeLists.txt.

You're also trying to *compile* with [various defines](https://github.com/kashirin-alex/hypertable/blob/master/src/cc/HyperPython/CMakeLists.txt#L52), but those are not going to work: `PYTHON_EXECUTABLE` is a CMake variable understood by the pybind11 CMake scripts, *not* a compile options.  Setting `PYPY_VERSION`/`PYTHON_VERSION`/`WITH_THREAD` is never something you should be doing manually: those should come from the Python/PyPy headers themselves.  (In this case, setting `PYPY_VERSION` is probably making pybind think it's compiling for PyPy, even though the available headers in the search path are actually for Python 2.7).

Suggested fix: you can simplify your `CMakeLists.txt` greatly if you take out the `find_package(Python)` from your root `CMakeLists.txt`, and instead use `find_package(pybind11)` coupled with [the provided cmake functions](http://pybind11.readthedocs.io/en/stable/compiling.html#building-with-cmake).  Let pybind11 find Python and extract the required include path from it.
