This became a long post so try and bear with me, but it describes defining four methods (other than `__init__`) which would allow us to handle all of the ACMEv1 vs. ACMEv2 differences that affect Certbot that I've identified.

I agree with Jacob that a class with all methods where the user still has to check the ACME version and call the right method probably doesn't buy us much. This could mean we skip my 2nd post of #5491 and just do the first, but if we wanted to try and do the class that tries to handle these differences, I'd do something like this:

Make this class be a simple wrapper for all methods in `ClientBase`. I'd probably do this through something like:
```
def __getattr__(self, name):
    if name in dir(ClientBase):
        return getattr(self.client, name)
    else:
        raise AttributeError
```
and document this behavior in the docstring for the class.

For the methods that try and handle the differences, we first add a method like `request_challenges` or `request_authorizations` that takes either a CSR or list of domains and returns a list of authorizations. A CSR is only necessary if `new_order` takes a CSR. For ACMEv1, it takes the domains (possibly after extracting them from the CSR) and calls `request_domain_challenges` for each domain and returns a list of obtained authz. For ACMEv2, this calls `new_order` with either the CSR/domains and returns the authorizations in the Order. This order object is stored as an attribute of the class to be handled internally

Next we add a method like `request_issuance` which takes a CSR and returns the cert and its chain. For ACMEv1, it forwards the arguments to `request_issuance` and then retries calling `fetch_chain` (see `certbot.client.obtain_certificate_from_csr`). For ACMEv2 it adds the CSR to the internal order object (if necessary) and calls `poll_order_and_request_issuance`. We then need to split the certificate and chain, probably by looking for the first line containing "END CERTIFICATE". If the `request_challenges`/`request_authorizations` method takes a CSR, we could keep this as an attribute of the class too and remove the parameter here if we really wanted.

The last piece of this is the ugliest. We need a method like `register`/`new_account` that also allows the user of the class to prompt the user to agree to the TOS. This method takes a `NewRegistration` object and a callback function. Luckily for us, we already use a callback function for agreeing to terms of service (see `certbot.client.register` and `certbot.main._determine_account`). If we're on ACMEv1, we call `register` with the `NewRegistration` object, pass the TOS into the callback which should either return a bool or raise an exception if the user doesn't agree, and then call `agree_to_tos`. For ACMEv2, we get the TOS out of the directory (perhaps by modifying `ClientV2` to have a property/method that does this for us), calls the callback with the TOS, sets `termsOfServiceAgreed` on the `NewRegistration`, and calls `new_account`. This method returns the up to date `Registration` object.

First and foremost, I really think we need to add `Listen` statements in order to migrate a HTTP only configurations to HTTPS. That said, this issue is still spot on, as in this case we're failing to actually deploy certificates in this kind of configuration. There are actually two different issues under this one. One for situations where user doesn't have an active HTTPS configuration, but would like to bind HTTPS to a non-standard port. The other one would be this exact issue you are facing.

As we need to define a port for Apache to use for the new HTTPS `VirtualHost`s, a possible fix for this particular issue would be:
 - Add a CLI parameter that allows user to define the port that HTTPS should be served on. We already have a functionality in place that checks if Apache listens to a specific port, and add a `Listen` statement if not. Currently this is fixed to `443` which is the reason behind this issue.

And perhaps for the configurations that already are serving HTTPS `VirtualHost`s:
 - If a HTTPS enabled `VirtualHost` is found anywhere in the active configuration, use port from that configuration for setting up the newly created `VirtualHost`s. If we're configuring certificates for pre-existing `VirtualHost`, skip the `Listen` statement addition completely.

I think probably doing something like that is a good idea if we can write the wrapper in a way that we don't lose much/any functionality. Otherwise, we basically have to write the code that handles the differences scattered throughout Certbot and the few other projects using the ACME library will have to do the same.

For calling it from `run`, it would be specifically for the case where we're reinstalling an existing cert. I suppose that could be called after calling `certonly`. Either way, we're going to be duplicating code in some manner. In practice, it will probably end up looking like moving all/some of the existing code into a helper function, but the `renew_deploy` has some sort of flag to blow away the existing managed lines.

So like for ciphersuites, once we've updated it once, we'll always be setting that in `renew_deploy`, because it's not like we're going to check for the old version. If we eventually end up updating everything, `renew_deploy` will end up always updating everything anyway.

Which, this removes the "doing less work" part of the reason for structuring it this way. I'm still good with having a separate method, because you can explicitly say "get rid of all this old stuff" rather than guessing about what you should do.

Yes. This is normal.

The purpose of the files in `/etc/letsencrypt/renewal` is to save the parameters that were used to obtain your certificate so we can use them again for renewal. These values can be overridden by:

1. Setting the values you want to be changed on the command line or a `cli.ini` file.
2. Modifying the value directly in `/etc/letsencrypt/renewal` like you did.

It's important to note, however, if you do option 1 and run `certbot renew`, it will modify the parameter in every file in `/etc/letsencrypt/renewal` that is due for renewal. This will be changed in the near future, but currently the only way to get finer grained control on which files are changes is to use the `certbot certonly` command.

More information about this is provided in our [renewal documentation](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#renewal).

I think this is a reasonable course to take, and 2048-bit dhparams is a good target. We can use the instructions at https://weakdh.org/sysadmin.html.

A couple things to keep in mind:
 - We want to avoid overwriting existing `ssl_dhparam` directives, like we do for other security directives.
 - Generating the dhparams may be quite slow on lower-powered machines. We should log clearly when we are about to do it (which would be just once on initial configuration, then we could store the resulting params in /etc/letsencrypt).

In terms of priority: Modern browsers will be negotiating ECDHE cipher suites, so aren't affected by the choice of dhparams. I think this would benefit a single-digit percentage of browsers. Still probably worthwhile IMO.

My guess would be that mod_rewrite isn't enabled, and we're not handling module activation in CentOS currently as the mechanism there is pretty stoic. @hex-m could you check your configuration, if it has 'LoadModule rewrite_module modules/mod_rewite.so' commented out somewhere.

``@factory.post_generation`` is executed *after* you object has been saved to the database.
In its code, ``self`` is the generated object, not a factory instance (there is a bit of black magic behind the scenes).

So you should be able to perform any operations you might want on that object, e.g:

```python
@factory.post_generation
def category(self, create, extracted, **kwargs):
    self.category = random.choice([tag.category for tag in self.tags.all()])
```

```Python
x_train, x_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    x, y, test_size=test_size, random_state=seed
)

tpot = TPOTRegressor(generations=1, population_size=20, verbosity=2, warm_start=True)
for _ in range(50):
    tpot.fit(x_train, y_train)
    y_predicted = tpot.predict(scaler_x.transform(x_test))
    print('me: ', mean_absolute_error(y_test, y_predicted))
    print('mse: ', mean_squared_error(y_test, y_predicted))

# score at the end of training
y_predicted = tpot.predict(scaler_x.transform(x_test))
print('me: ', mean_absolute_error(y_test, y_predicted))
print('mse: ', mean_squared_error(y_test, y_predicted))
```

> Do I need to also include the training script (examples/imagenet do not apply here, I think)?

> Should I reuse some of the blocks of the existing Densenet implementation (At work I'm using some custom blocks)

> Should I train the model using the same hyperparameters and dataset as the original paper?

If you have something that produces better results you can use that, but be sure to evaluate and provide the performance metrics on a standardized evaluation dataset and the code in your PR should enable others to reproduce the model.

I decided to refactor `_process_task` to make the error handling a little cleaner, and I moved storage of the dummy output to its caller. It now always stores a valid dummy output, even if the task produced an exception. `_process_task` still gets all arguments, including the dummy output. I guess it's not totally necessary since the dummy objects get pinned anyway, but it's nice to have a sanity check that they are actually there when the task begins to execute.

I agree that we should try to avoid special casing for actors, but it's hard to avoid with the current structure. A better way to handle it would probably be to make an `ActorWorker` that is a subclass of `Worker`, but that would be much a larger refactor effort.

@istoica @raulchen 
 - performance between remote lambda vs binary rewrite. We don't have a performance comparison between remote lambda and binary rewrite. But you may look [here](https://dzone.com/articles/measuring-the-serialization-performance-of-lambdas). A one time cost of remote lambda may not be enough, as we don't find a way to get a key of the lambda function so that we can cache and fetch the lambda.
 - in SINGLE_PRORCESS mode (provided for our application developers so they don't both non-java libraries or processes or envs), the worker runs purely in Java. We do have a version in multiple threads, but right now we have a simpler version in one single thread. 
 - IDE compatability about annotation processor. I don't really test it myself,  and according to @raulchen, there won't be much problem, as you expected. However, I may still not feel very comfortable about ```RemoteFoo``` because this is against my (as a programmer) type completeness intuition during programming (esp. when the whole thing is transparent). Instead, I would prefer to have an explicit process to generate the initial version of ```RemoteFoo``` and then the compiler helps maintain the consistence between ```Foo``` and ```RemoteFoo```. That's said, I'm not a typical Java programmer, so this really depends on the community's taste.

Apologies for the late response @pkittenis, I've been sick for the past few days. I've elaborated below on what I was attempting to explain earlier, and have put some general suggested changes first, the combination of which is hopefully clearer than what I wrote previously.

## General Issue
Both `run_command` and `exec_command` currently make problematic assumptions about the environment of the remote system; some of these are due to bugs, but even with those fixed the `shell`, `sudo`, and `user` arguments cannot be implemented in a way that is actually universal/generic across all remote systems. I suspect that the attempt to implement their functionality inside `run/exec_command` is what lead to some of the bugs.


## Suggestions
1) Remove the `shell`, `sudo`, and `user` arguments from `run_command` and `exec_command` and their callers where relevant, as well as removing all existing escaping and quoting mechanisms -- just pass the supplied command through as-is, because the caller is really the only one who can possibly know enough about the remote system to construct it appropriately.
2) Add helper functions for doing commonly-useful things like Bourne-compatible shell escaping and quoting, wrapping commands in `sudo` or `su`, etc.
3) Implement functions like `run_unix_command` and `exec_unix_command` (or `run_bourne_command`/`exec_bourne_command`, but I suspect this would be less intuitive to people) that behave like the current functions (but with fixes for some of the below issues, where possible), in order to make porting existing uses trivial, and use the helpers from suggestion 2 in them

Moving them into their own function(s) would also make it easier to cleanly implement variants of the functionality for other types of remote system.

## Specific Problems

### For the paramiko-backed `exec_command`

#### Quoting Issue
How a command is run *on the remote system* does not _not_ vary based on the client-side SSH library used; the OpenSSH server will _always_ use the remote user's shell to run a command given on an exec request (on Linux it determines what the shell is by checking user's passwd entry, possibly resolving it through NSS, and as far as I can tell you can't configure it at run-time to do otherwise). Other SSH daemons may do other things.

I would hazard a guess that you were seeing `command not found` for an `echo something` test when specifying `use_shell=False`, which would be because this results in (essentially) the following sequence of events:
    1. Internally, `command = 'echo something'`, `_command = '"%(command)s"'`
    2. This gets passed along to paramiko's `exec_command`, which starts a non-interactive, non-login ssh session to run the command -- the equivalent of `ssh user@host '"echo something"`
    3. OpenSSH's `sshd` starts the remote shell, here assumed to be a Bourne-compatible shell, and attempts to run the given exec string -- the equivalent of the following in bash or Bourne shell: `$ "echo something"`
    4. The command is not found, because the entire thing is quoted, meaning it gets passed in as a *single token* to the underlying shell, and of course there is no builtin function or binary in `$PATH` that happens to be called "echo something"

If the function weren't falling back to quoting the command string, it would work just fine because it would be correctly tokenized by the remote shell into two tokens (a command and an argument), and it would call the built-in echo function:
```
$ python
Python 3.6.4 (default, Dec 19 2017, 05:36:13)
[GCC 7.3.0] on linux
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> from paramiko import SSHClient, WarningPolicy
>>> sshc = SSHClient()
>>> sshc.set_missing_host_key_policy(WarningPolicy())
>>> sshc.connect('192.168.8.8', username='pssh_testing', port=54201)
/nix/store/5rkyjz5jbff55x6ssnfycgs3kh0vkha8-python3-3.6.4-env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/paramiko/client.py:682: UserWarning: Unknown ssh-rsa host key for [192.168.8.8]:54201: b'cfce84261b6671ceb8de9ac9521c00db'
  (key.get_name(), hostname, hexlify(key.get_fingerprint())))
>>> stdin, stdout, stderr = sshc.exec_command('echo --help')
>>> stdout.read()
b'--help\n'
>>>
```
vs:
```
>>> stdin, stdout, stderr = sshc.exec_command('"echo --help"')
>>> stdout.read()
b''
>>> stderr.read()
b'bash: echo --help: command not found\n'
>>>
```

Note: I used `echo --help` for the command string, because **if** the remote `echo` being used were a standalone binary rather than a shell builtin it would have actually printed out a help page rather than echoing `--help` back at me, e.g.:
```
shados@dreamlogic[/home/shados] λ ssh pssh_testing@s2 'echo --help'
--help
shados@dreamlogic[/home/shados] λ ssh pssh_testing@s2
[pssh_testing@home ~]$ echo --help
--help
[pssh_testing@home ~]$ /usr/bin/echo --help
Usage: /usr/bin/echo [SHORT-OPTION]... [STRING]...
  or:  /usr/bin/echo LONG-OPTION
Echo the STRING(s) to standard output.

  -n             do not output the trailing newline
  -e             enable interpretation of backslash escapes

...

  \xHH    byte with hexadecimal value HH (1 to 2 digits)

NOTE: your shell may have its own version of echo, which usually supersedes
the version described here.  Please refer to your shell's documentation
for details about the options it supports.

GNU coreutils online help: <https://www.gnu.org/software/coreutils/>
Full documentation at: <https://www.gnu.org/software/coreutils/echo>
or available locally via: info '(coreutils) echo invocation'
[pssh_testing@home ~]$
```


#### Escaping Issue
The [command escaping mechanism](https://github.com/ParallelSSH/parallel-ssh/blob/db0de23b77dfc95da10075cb05f3d2cc43c29156/pssh/ssh_client.py#L277-L278) is also problematic:
- It assumes both that the remote shell will be a Bourne-compatible shell, *and* that the command string is intended to be passed as a single token to another command (a specified shell or sudo), because otherwise you probably would want the command string to be unescaped so that it would be interpreted by the remote shell.  
You may want some part of the command string to be unescaped even if you are passing it to another shell -- you may want variables that only exist in the user's default shell substituted in, for example.
- There's no way to disable it, making the function incompatible with any device or configuration that isn't serving a Bourne-compatible shell on the remote end.
- I'm also not entirely confident that it is escaping properly, but I admit I haven't given it enough thought or testing to be sure either way. That said, Python 3.3 added `shlex.quote`, and it is [small/simple enough](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/61eda7260ac9fcdb6c5f066eb48dc344544bdfa6/Lib/shlex.py#L308-L319) to reasonbly add to parallel-ssh if you do want to include Bourne-compatible shell escaping in the library.

### For the Native Client's `run_command`
If none of `sudo`, `user`, or `shell` are specified then the command string is [passed through completely unmodified (not quoted)](https://github.com/ParallelSSH/parallel-ssh/blob/db0de23b77dfc95da10075cb05f3d2cc43c29156/pssh/ssh2_client.py#L359-L370), which is fine.

But:
1) If any of them are specified then the command will take the form `%(shell)s '%(command)s'` or `sudo -u %(user)s -S %(shell)s '%(command)s'`, this essentially makes use of those arguments incompatible with any devices or configurations that do not support Bourne-compatible quoting syntax, or that only have `su` and not `sudo` (if you don't need to supply a password, `su` also works just fine in non-interactive ssh session).
The user could implement this themselves by preparing an appropriate `command` and avoiding these arguments, but then why advertise the functionality at the level of this function instead of on another more specific one, or as command-formatting/wrapping helper functions?
2) If `sudo` is specified and `shell` is not, you should pass `-s` to `sudo` instead of using `$SHELL` as the command -- it is a little more robust, as if `$SHELL` is not set it will look up the user's shell in the passwd database (on appropriately-configured Linux it will even resolve their passwd db entry via NSS).
3) It is not necessary to have `sudo` use a shell to run the command at all, you can just pass it the **unquoted** command to have `sudo` call it directly.
4) Unlike in the paramiko-backed `exec_command`, there's no attempt at escaping the passed command string -- this is not a problem in itself (I mean, the whole point I'm arguing is that escaping and quoting shouldn't be done at this level -- the caller may even want variables evaluated in the ssh user's shell, not the specified shell), but it's bizarre that the behaviour is inconsistent between the two functions.

@mpaget proper design doc for this is coming, it's easier to document a working prototype. But to give an idea of what this all about here is a brief overview:

You can think of usual `datacube.load` as 2 step process

1. Build 3d VRT (virtual raster) for a given user query (space,time, product)
2. Load that VRT into memory at a requested projection/resolution resulting in `xarray.Dataset`

What we call "virtual products" generalise this to support

1. Mixing of multiple on-disk products (NBAR + PQ for example)
2. Applying user supplied data transforms at load time (e.g. (NBAR+PQ)->NBAR_clear_pixels_only)
3. Treating multiple similar product as one (e.g. NBAR from LS5,LS7,LS8)

Why?

- Increased memory/load time efficiency when dealing with multi-product workflows
  - No need to load data that will be discarded (e.g. missing PQ for a given NBAR tile)
  - Transforming data time slice by time slice reduces peak memory requirement compared to current approach of loading all source data followed by a transform.
- Better UX and code re-use. 
  - User will be able to just say `clear_pixel(product='ls5_nbar_albers')` rather manually loading two product and reconciling data post-load.

Thanks for reporting @DmPanov . The problem is that LocalStack expects a different URL for the invocation of the API gateway method.

Instead of calling this URL:
```
/restapis/1014462339/resources/77117520A-Z8
```
... you need to be calling this URL:
```
/restapis/1014462339/resources/_user_request_/77117520A-Z8
```
This is a limitation caused by the fact that all endpoints are bound to `localhost` and the system has no way of knowing whether the invocation is (1) a request to update the API gateway method resource, or (2) actually invoke the API gateway method.

One possible solution is to set up a small reverse proxy that listens on a different port and forwards any requests on `/restapis/1014462339/resources/77117520A-Z8` to `/restapis/1014462339/resources/_user_request_/77117520A-Z8` on the LocalStack API Gateway port (port 4567).

See here for reference: https://github.com/localstack/localstack/blob/master/localstack/services/apigateway/apigateway_listener.py#L161
https://github.com/localstack/localstack/blob/master/localstack/constants.py#L55

@abe-mart Values assigned to GEKKO variables are communicated through the csv file. If csv_read = 0, only scalar values of parameters and variables are read through the .apm file. If csv_read=1, all parameter values are also read in. If csv_read=2, all parameter and (initial) variable values are loaded.
The default GEKKO value is 2.
Only values defined by the user since model initialization or the last solve are written to the csv. All other values are loaded back from the APM internal .t0 database files and timeshifted according to the time_shift option.
If a measurement (.meas) is defined, the associated variable value is not written to the csv. Also, if a variable value is fixed with the fix() command, the fixed value is written in the csv file.

When you call `writer` are you giving it just the data dictionary? The documentation should probably be a bit more clear (and maybe there's a way to add some check to the incoming data), but the `records` argument should be an iterable of data dictionaries (typically this is something like a list, but it should be able to be a tuple or a generator or something else like that).

As a side note, debugging can be a little awkward because of the layout of this library. There is both a cython implementation and a pure python implementation. When running under the PyPy interpreter, the pure python is used (so the files like `_write_py.py` whereas on the CPython interpreter the cython version is used (files like `_write.pyx`). If you wanted to debug, the easiest way would probably be to go to the fastavro install folder and remove the compiled cython files (either `.so`, `.pyd`, or maybe something else). This will force it to use the pure python (and your stack trace should change from having `_write.pyx` to `_write_py.py`) and then you would be able to use `pdb`
